stem: Which of the following are considered roles and responsibilities of the Board of Certification (BOC) for athletic training? Select all that apply.
choices: Developing the certification exam for candidates who want to become athletic trainers
Performing an annual review on each athletic trainer’s competence
Defining the standards for the continuing education requirements for athletic trainers
Taking disciplinary action if an athletic trainer violates professional responsibilities
Determining salary ranges for athletic trainers based on their years of experience
rationale: The Board of Certification (BOC) for athletic trainers is responsible for certifying athletic trainers by providing an exam that must be passed to receive the credential. In addition, athletic trainers must regularly complete continuing education units (CEUs) to stay informed on the latest science and best practices in the field. The BOC is responsible for providing national standards on CEUs and offering continuing education opportunities based on the requirements. The BOC provides a Code of Professional Responsibility for athletic trainers, and they may enforce disciplinary action for any individual who does not comply with the responsibilities outlined in the Code. The BOC does not evaluate each athletic trainer’s individual competence, and salary ranges are determined by a variety of factors outside the BOC’s authority, such as the industry or the employer.

stem: What are some of the dangers of using anabolic steroids? Select all that apply.
rationale: The use of steroids is illegal without documented medical necessity. Steroids can have dangerous side effects, especially for teenagers. Stunted growth is one important side effect. The bones mature faster than normal, sending a signal to the body to stop growing. Steroid use can lead to infertility in both men and women. In men, there can be a loss of sperm and a reduction in testicle size. In women, steroid use can lead to disruption of the menstrual cycle. Steroids can lead to enlargement of the heart, certain types of cancer, muscle aches, and hair loss. Steroids can cause elevations in blood cholesterol levels that can lead to heart disease. Steroids can also lead to a change in behavior, characterized by violence, mood swings, and aggression. Most athletes in college, Olympic, and professional sports will need to undergo drug testing to monitor for steroid abuse. Athletes found to be abusing steroids face legal consequences.

stem: An athletic trainer plans to use spinal mobilization techniques as part of a patient’s therapeutic plan. Which condition would be a contraindication for these techniques?
rationale: Osteoporosis is characterized by decreased bone density, resulting in bone fragility and increased risk of fracture. Spinal manipulations should not be used in osteoporosis because the manipulations can put excess stress on the bones; however, spinal manipulations could be useful in promoting proper alignment and reducing pain in the rest of the listed conditions.

stem: A head wrestling coach is working with the athletic trainer to enact cleaning and disinfecting precautions for all mats and equipment so that they can better prevent the spread of disease among the wrestling athletes. Which of the following diseases should the coach and trainer try to prevent through these cleaning and disinfecting measures? Select all that apply.
rationale: MRSA (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus) and impetigo are both skin infections that are commonly spread through close contact, which is a significant component of wrestling. Proper cleaning procedures can reduce or prevent these infections from being transmitted. Auricular hematoma, or ‘cauliflower ear,’ is a common wrestling injury, but it is caused by direct trauma to the ear and would not be transmitted through contact with skin or wrestling mats. Psoriasis and eczema are chronic skin conditions that are not transferred through person-to-person contact or touching an infected surface.

stem: During a high school basketball training camp, a boy complains of knee pain after landing from a jump shot. He reports hearing a popping sound when the injury occurred. Which of the following is the most likely explanation?
rationale: Injuries to the anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) are common in athletes participating in basketball, skiing, soccer, and football. Injury to the ACL typically involves a hit to the side of the knee, landing from a jump, hyperextending the knee, pivoting the knee, or a quick change in direction when running. The common symptom that helps to distinguish this injury is a popping sound made at the time of injury. This sound occurs as the ACL is torn either partially or completely. Significant knee swelling will occur within a few hours of injury. Initial treatment should include elevating the leg so that the knee joint is above the heart, applying ice, and administering pain relievers such as ibuprofen. The patient should be directed to obtain medical care from a licensed physician.

stem: Which of the following is an example of a psychosocial approach to helping a patient manage an injury?
choices: Scheduling regular rehabilitation sessions for the patient
Fostering a positive attitude in the patient
Educating the patient on medications for pain relief
Evaluating the risk of re-injury from functional testing
rationale: Psychosocial approaches address psychological and social aspects of managing an injury, such as the patient's emotional reactions to the injury. Fostering a positive attitude in an injured athlete is one example of a psychosocial approach, as the positive attitude can help the patient manage the stress of injury and stay motivated during the rehabilitation process.

stem: Which of the following injuries are common in long distance runners? Select all that apply.
choices: Stress fracture of the lower leg or foot
rationale: Athletic trainers who work with long-distance runners need to be aware of injuries common to this sport. Most injuries involve the hip, leg, or foot. Shin splints typically refers to pain that involves the area of the tibia. It can be related to muscle, bone, or the attachment of the muscle to the bone. A stress fracture is a microscopic break that is caused by repetitive force on an area such as the foot, lower leg, tibia, or fibula. Achilles tendonitis is inflammation of the large tendon that is located in the back of the ankle. Iliotibial band friction syndrome is pain in the knee that worsens with running or stepping up or down stairs. It is commonly referred to as runner’s knee.

stem: An athletic trainer suspects that a 19-year-old female gymnast might have an eating disorder. She reveals that she is being treated by student health services, but she does not disclose the reason. The athletic trainer calls student health services and talks to the physician, but the physician declines to provide any information regarding the patient’s medical condition. What affords the physician this right?
choices: Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA)
Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
rationale: The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) is a federal law that was enacted in 1996 to protect the privacy of consumers in matters pertaining to health information. The law has other purposes besides privacy: it covers health care providers, health insurance plans, and health care clearinghouses. Unless there is a signed, written release specifically stating that personal health information may be provided to athletic trainers or coaching staff, the physician is not obligated to comply with the request. Given that the patient is over the age of 18, even her parents would not be able to obtain information unless written authorization is provided. Many schools now require student athletes to sign releases authorizing the release of medical information in order to participate in a sport. HIPAA is a confusing law in regards to college athletics, but it is important to understand how it impacts the role of an athletic trainer.

stem: A foam roller is an example of what type of therapeutic modality?
rationale: A foam roller is a device used in myofascial release. It can be used by patients to perform self-massage for soft tissues in various muscle groups. An athletic trainer may perform this modality but would likely use their own hands. Soft tissues include muscle, tendons, ligaments, and the fascia. Foam rollers come in various sizes. The patient can roll the device over an area 10 times to help loosen up an area that may be tight. Common areas include lower and upper back, hamstring, gluteus, hip rotators, and shoulders. It can also be used to warm up or cool down following exercise. The benefits of this therapeutic modality include improving the range of motion of various joints, relieving muscle pain and soreness, and helping to maintain the normal function of the muscle length.

stem: Which of the following scenarios violate(s) National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) guidelines? Select all that apply.
choices: A football player with an artificial leg
A basketball player’s wrist covered with half an inch of foam and tape
A pole-vaulter using a forearm cover
A softball pitcher with a neutral-colored cast on her non-pitching arm
A volleyball player with newly pierced ears who has taped her ears in preparation for a game
rationale: The National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) has rules for each sport, and athletic trainers need to be informed and knowledgeable about these rules. The use of unpermitted equipment poses injury risks to student athletes. Many rules pertain to the mandatory use of protective equipment, such as helmets, to prevent injuries. Artificial limbs are not allowed for football players because of the potential to cause harm to other players. A basketball player can play with certain injuries involving the arm area as long as the material used to cover the injury is soft and not likely to cause harm. A pole-vaulter may use a forearm protector. In softball, various types of casts and splints are allowed, but they must be well padded for the protection of all players. The pitcher must wear a cast that is neutral in color so that it does not distract other players. All jewelry is prohibited in volleyball, and jewelry must not be taped.

stem: Which of the following symptoms may be indicative of the female triad in a female athlete? Select all that apply.
rationale: The female triad is the term used to describe disordered eating that leads to the development of amenorrhea and osteoporosis. The disordered eating (DE) does not have to be a fully diagnosed eating disorder, such as anorexia nervosa, but the female triad begins with DE. Disordered eating typically involves not eating enough calories in an attempt to be thin. Weight loss is the first clue. Significant weight loss can lead to amenorrhea, which is when menstruation stops. When this occurs for a prolonged period, it can lead to loss of bone mass or osteoporosis due to lack of estrogen. Osteoporosis can be further complicated by inadequate calcium intake. Other symptoms of DE may include dehydration, frequent urination, cold intolerance, inability to focus, and dental problems. It is important to monitor the menstrual cycles of female athletes and to focus on health and nutrition rather than weight and body size.

stem: A high school football athlete sustains a knee injury while attempting an interception. The athletic trainer begins to immediately assess the injury and provide care. The athletic trainer sees that the athlete’s knee is swollen. In what area of SOAP notes would this observation be included?
rationale: The observation of a swollen knee would go under the objective findings of SOAP notes. Objective information is what the athletic trainer can observe or measure. The injured patient provides subjective information, including pain levels and sensations felt during injury. Assessment involves the athletic trainer’s best professional hypothesis on the nature and extent of the injury from the subjective and objective information gathered. The plan area in SOAP notes details what treatment was provided and necessary next steps.

stem: A college athlete has a recurrent stress fracture in her foot due to long distance running. A bone density test reveals a T-score of 2.2, indicating osteopenia. A registered dietitian recommends a high- calcium diet. Which of the following foods are good sources of calcium? Select all that apply.
rationale: Calcium is an important mineral in bone growth and repair. It has many other functions in the body as well, including regulation of blood pressure and blood clotting. A bone density test is a test that uses x- rays to determine the presence of osteoporosis and the overall risk of fracture. Osteoporosis is the thinning of the bones over time, and osteopenia is the condition in which the bones have started to thin but have not progressed to the more advanced form of osteoporosis. Risk factors for osteoporosis include poor calcium intake, poor overall eating habits, low body weight, smoking, female gender, Caucasian or Asian ethnicity, and certain medications. Increasing calcium in the diet is one way to help treat osteopenia. High-calcium foods include dairy products, tofu, and dark green leafy vegetables. Weight-bearing exercise is also recommended, as well as refraining from smoking or excessive alcohol use.

stem: In this situation, the athletic trainer should FIRST:
choices: Check the athlete’s health record for any pre-existing conditions.
Prepare to move the injured athlete from the ice.
Assess if the athlete is conscious.
Designate someone to bring an AED to the scene.
Remove the athlete’s facemask in preparation for providing CPR.
rationale: The first step in any on-the-field emergency care assessment is to assess the athlete’s level of consciousness. This assessment will guide the next decisions. Anyone who is unconscious should be considered to have a life-threatening injury, and this necessitates calling 911 as the next step. Checking the athlete’s health record would provide an unnecessary delay in the emergency response, and the other three steps could be unnecessary or even dangerous depending on what is found upon further assessment.
scenario: A hockey athlete collides with a member of the opposing team during a playoff game, slamming into the boards. The athlete falls to the ice and he appears to be motionless as the athletic trainer approaches.

stem: The athletic trainer’s assessment reveals that the patient is conscious and breathing. The patient reports sharp, intense pain on one side of his neck. The athlete's helmet has shifted as a result of the impact, forcing his head slightly to the opposite side. The athletic trainer suspects a cervical spine injury or whiplash. Emergency services have arrived and would like to transport the athlete to the hospital for further evaluation. If the athletic trainer decides to proceed with moving the patient off the ice, what is the next step?
choices: With the help of other responders, logroll the athlete onto a spine board.
Remove the shoulder pad on the side of the suspected injury and check for visible deformity.
Assist the athlete in sitting up and evaluate him for 30 seconds before assisting him to standing.
With the help of other responders, remove the athlete’s helmet and shoulder pads while stabilizing the cervical spine.
Prepare to elevate the athlete’s legs to prevent shock.
rationale: If the athlete is wearing protective equipment such as a helmet and shoulder pads (as he would be wearing in hockey) but they have shifted or otherwise do not allow the spine to be in a neutral position, both helmet and shoulder pads should be removed while providing cervical spine stabilization. This provides easier access for emergency care while minimizing further injury. Note that if the helmet and shoulder pads are in place and the athlete's airway is not obstructed, it is generally better to leave the protective equipment on until the athlete arrives at the emergency medical facility. A properly fitted helmet can be immobilized so that it in turn keeps the head in position without risking further injury while removing it. Furthermore, the cold of a hockey arena can contribute to shock, so removing any more equipment or clothing than necessary should be avoided. The athlete should not be logrolled with shoulder pads on, nor should only one shoulder pad be removed, as these actions could compromise the cervical spine. The patient should not be assisted in sitting up, since the extent of the injury is not clear, and stabilizing the patient is the first priority over shock prevention.

stem: What type of upper extremity injury might the hockey athlete have suffered based on the available information? Select all that apply.
rationale: Blunt force trauma, such as a direct collision, can cause various upper extremity injuries, including a fracture of the humerus, glenohumeral joint sprain, or acromioclavicular sprain. Shoulder impingement is due to repetitive movement, and the mechanism of scapular dyskinesis is repetitive use with abnormal scapular positioning or biomechanics. Neither of these are acute injuries from trauma.

stem: If the hockey athlete’s injuries include a sprain of the shoulder ligaments, what will be a primary objective of rehabilitation? Select all that apply.
choices: Prolonging the inflammatory response phase in the affected joint
Improving the action of osteoblasts in the affected joint
Strengthening the muscles surrounding the affected joint
Restoring stability to the affected joint
Increasing range of motion in the ligaments surrounding the affected joint
rationale: The nature of such a sprain injury involves stretching or tearing of ligaments, compromising stability and strength at the joint; therefore, two main objectives would be strengthening the surrounding muscles and restoring stability. While the inflammatory process must occur as part of tissue healing, prolonging the inflammatory phase is not a rehabilitation objective. Osteoblasts are responsible for building new bone and would not be involved in a ligament injury. Since the ligaments are already compromised by injury, stretching them further by increasing range of motion is not a rehabilitation objective.

stem: As the hockey athlete progresses in his injury rehabilitation, the athletic trainer chooses to include upper body closed kinetic chain exercises. Which of the following exercises would be classified as closed kinetic chain? Select all that apply.
choices: Weight shifts while on the hands
Y, T, and Ws with light dumbbells
rationale: A closed kinetic chain exercise or movement is when the distal extremities (in this case, the hands) are in contact with a surface or on the ground to bear weight, as would be the case with weight shifts on the hands and wall pushups. Conversely, in an open kinetic chain exercise or movement, the distal extremities are not in contact with a surface where they are bearing weight. This can include physically holding or tossing a free weight; therefore, supine ball toss and Y, T, and Ws are classified as open kinetic chain. The sleeper stretch is also not a closed kinetic chain movement, as the hands are not affixed to a surface for weight-bearing.

stem: Which of the following are methods used for assessing body composition? Select all that apply.
rationale: Anthropometry, the measurement of a patient’s height and weight, is commonly done in fitness testing; however, what is important is the assessment of body composition, which cannot be done with a scale. Body weight is composed, in part, of adipose (or fat) tissue and muscle. Many athletes have an increased level of muscle mass. Methods used to assess body composition are skinfold analysis, underwater weighing, and bioelectrical impedance. BMI is an equation that has been determined to be ineffective at correctly assessing body composition and is no longer recommended as a diagnostic tool. Skinfold analysis is completed using skinfold calipers that directly measure the thickness of the skin when gently pulled away from the body at certain points. Equations are then used to convert those measurements into body compositions. Underwater weighing involves the use of lung volume, the patient’s weight when measured underwater, and an equation. Bioelectrical impedance measures the electrical resistance between electrodes placed on various points on the body. Body fat will resist electricity, and body fat percentage can be calculated based on this data.

stem: A distance runner on the track team is experiencing Achilles tendon pain and stiffness. Which of the following could be part of her therapeutic intervention? Select all that apply.
choices: Circuit training that avoids repetitive tendon motion
rationale: Objectives for managing Achilles tendinopathies include reducing or eliminating the repetitive overload causing stress on the tendon. Symptoms can be increased if the gastrocnemius and/or soleus lack flexibility, so stretching each of these may be a component of therapeutic intervention. Maintaining fitness levels while minimizing tendon stress is also an objective, so the athletic trainer may have the patient engage in exercises such as bicycling or circuit training. Plyometrics would not be used, as forceful, explosive movement could put excess stress on the already inflamed Achilles tendon.

stem: An athletic trainer is using the overhead squat test with patients and observes that some patients exhibit lumbar lordosis. Muscles that are likely excessively tight in these patients include which of the following? Select all that apply.
rationale: Observations in the overhead squat test may include observing that body segments are pulled out of neutral alignment. Muscles that are excessively tight tend to pull in the direction of tightness. A patient with lumbar lordosis likely has tight psoas and erector spinae muscles, as these are pulling the pelvis forward out of neutral alignment.

stem: An athlete gets hit in the mouth by a softball and loses a front tooth. A teammate has located the tooth. Which of the following are appropriate next steps? Select all that apply.
choices: Scrub the tooth to remove the dirt, and then reinsert it into the socket.
Put the tooth into saline solution or an emergency tooth preservation kit.
Place the tooth in a cup of cold whole milk.
Wrap the tooth in clean gauze and give it to the athlete’s parents.
Ask the athlete to hold the tooth in the mouth, between the cheek and gum.
rationale: If an athlete sustains an injury to the mouth that results in a tooth being knocked out, the best chance of saving the tooth is to replace the tooth in its socket within 30 minutes. The tooth should only be handled by its crown, not the roots. The tooth should be gently rinsed off with water but not scrubbed. It should then be placed in an emergency tooth preservation kit, cold milk, or saline solution. If none of these options is available, the tooth should be placed in the athlete’s mouth between the cheek and gum. The main point is to keep the tooth moist at all times, and never wrapped in tissue or gauze which would expedite it drying out. Once appropriately storing the tooth, the athlete should be brought to the nearest emergency department for care.

stem: An athletic trainer and a strength & conditioning coach are preparing a team of volleyball athletes to go through preseason assessments of strength, flexibility, and power. Which of the following would enhance the reliability of these assessments? Select all that apply.
choices: Providing a standardized warm-up to all athletes being tested
Maintaining consistent rest times for all athletes between assessments
Performing assessments at various times of day throughout the season
Having the athletes evaluate each other on the tests for time efficiency
Giving specific pre-assessment instructions to athletes on the days leading up to the assessments
rationale: Reliability refers to the consistency of a test and is a measure of whether the test will produce similar results when repeated. Reliability is important for this scenario to get accurate preseason measurements that can then be compared or used to identify areas of improvement. To maximize reliability, assessment procedures should be as standardized as possible. This can include a standardized warm-up and consistent rest times. Athletes should also know that assessments are coming, since factors like sleep, nutrition, and hydration can affect results. Performing assessments at various times or having athletes test each other could detract from the accuracy and consistency of the assessments.

stem: A lacrosse athlete has been acting irritable, anxious, and disinterested in participating in practice. The athletic trainer suspects that the athlete is experiencing overtraining. The athlete is also more likely to experience which of the following? Select all that apply.
rationale: In overtraining, there is an imbalance between an athlete’s ability to cope and the physical loads placed on the athlete. Overtraining can result from inadequate rest and recovery or emotional stress and can lead to staleness and burnout. An athlete experiencing overtraining is likely to experience a higher heart rate, higher blood pressure, and increased risk of overuse injury. The parasympathetic system is associated with states of relaxation in the body, such as resting, digestion, and conserving energy; therefore, this system would not be more active when experiencing the stress of overtraining. Serotonin is a neurotransmitter that affects mood. Higher levels promote feelings of happiness and well-being, so this also would not be increased in overtraining.

stem: A patient with known diabetes mellitus has a hypoglycemic reaction during an athletic event. What should be done to begin treatment?
choices: Have the patient drink a quart of orange juice and return to the game.
Have the patient eat a sandwich.
Have the patient drink 4 ounces of juice or soda, then reassess in 15 minutes.
rationale: Hypoglycemia is defined as a blood sugar level less than 70 mg/dL. Symptoms of hypoglycemia include shaking, cold sweats, blurry vision, headache, hunger, irritability, and weakness. Most patients with diabetes will be able to recognize the initial symptoms and will know they need to address the issue immediately. If untreated, this can lead to seizures, unconsciousness, or coma. Treatment typically consists of 4 ounces of juice or soda, 5 or 6 hard candies, or about a tablespoon of sugar and then waiting 15 minutes to see if the blood sugar rises. If it does not, additional help may be needed. Once the blood sugar is in the normal range, a healthy snack should be eaten to help sustain the blood sugar level. It is important not to over-treat a hypoglycemic reaction with too much sugar, because this can cause additional issues if the blood sugar is too high.

stem: An athletic trainer is planning a rehabilitation program for a patient with chronic low back pain. Which of the following would be most appropriate in the initial stages of the program? Select all that apply.
rationale: While chronic low back pain may not have one single cause, patients with chronic low back pain will typically benefit from exercises that promote learning to stabilize the trunk and spine, such as the dying bug, the drawing-in maneuver to co-contract the transverse abdominus and multifidus, and glute bridging. Since the Romanian deadlift and leg press use weight as external resistance, they would not be appropriate in the initial stages of a low back pain program. The patient needs to learn core stabilization with only bodyweight movements first to retrain proper patterns.

stem: An athletic trainer is assisting the head football coach in selecting footwear for all athletes on the football team. Which of the following considerations would apply? Select all that apply.
choices: Take multiple measurements when fitting athletes for shoes.
Fit athletes with shoes at the beginning of the day.
Measure both feet to obtain correct sizing.
Choose shoes with smooth soles to increase acceleration.
Use footwear that will permit the insertion of orthotics.
rationale: Shoes for athletes must fit properly to minimize injury risk and maximize performance. Fitting athletes for shoes should involve measuring from the heel to the metatarsophalangeal joint as well as the distance from the heel to the longest toe, with the longer of the two measurements used for shoe fitting. For the most customized fit, both feet should be measured, as they may differ in size. Shoes should be fitted at the end of the day, not at the beginning. Since football is a sport requiring change of direction, smooth shoes would put an athlete at risk of injury. Cleated shoes would be preferable. Orthotics are only needed if a patient’s feet have biomechanical issues, so footwear that can accommodate orthotics would not be needed for the entire team.

stem: A 25-year-old man is training for his first marathon. He was diagnosed with type 1, insulin- dependent diabetes when he was 18 years old. He is done stretching and is getting ready to start his run. He appears sweaty, shaky, and sluggish, but he brushes it off saying he didn’t sleep well last night. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial response to this situation? Select all that apply.
choices: Accept his explanation and allow him to run.
Suggest he take a dose of insulin before running due to high blood sugar.
Ask additional questions about his most recent insulin dose and meal, due to low blood sugar.
Prevent him from running until his blood glucose level has been checked and treated accordingly.
rationale: Hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) is a common side effect of insulin because blood glucose stability is dependent on so many different factors. These factors include insulin dosage, timing and composition of meals, exercise level, and more. Signs of a hypoglycemic reaction include shaking, sweating, and hunger. Later signs can include confusion, fatigue, appearing intoxicated, and seizures that can lead to unconsciousness. Exercise has a direct effect on blood glucose levels as well. Hypoglycemia is commonly seen and can occur before, during, or after exercise. High levels of exercise can accelerate a low blood sugar reaction. It is important to monitor blood glucose levels prior to exercising to determine if a snack or meal is needed. An athletic trainer needs to be familiar with the signs of hypoglycemia, especially if working with patients with a known history of any type of diabetes.

stem: Which of the following is a type of PNF stretching technique? Select all that apply.
rationale: There are various types of PNF (proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation) techniques, all of which involve contraction and relaxation of both agonist and antagonist muscles using cycles of a 10-second active push phase and a 10-second passive relaxation phase. In contract-relax, the targeted muscle group is isotonically contracted during the active push phase. In slow-reversal-hold-relax, the agonist muscle is contracted while passive tension is applied to stretch the relaxed antagonist muscle. In hold-relax, the targeted muscle group is contracted isometrically against resistance during the active push phase. Hold-contract, contract-flex-extend, and stretch-contract are not PNF stretching techniques.

stem: In the initial treatment of a patient with a suspected spinal cord injury, which of the following is an appropriate step to take? Select all that apply.
choices: Ensure open airway, adequate breathing, and circulation.
Stabilize the neck with a soft cervical collar.
Use a scoop stretcher if the athlete needs to be transported.
Ask the athlete to move the neck through normal range of motion.
Stabilize the neck with a rigid collar.
rationale: In the case of a suspected spinal cord injury, the first step is to always ensure that the patient is breathing. The ABCs—airway, breathing, and circulation—should be verified. It is imperative that the neck and spine be immobilized to prevent additional damage. The neck should be stabilized with a rigid cervical collar. A soft collar would be used for comfort in conditions like a stiff neck where muscle guarding is present, it would not be used in an acute emergency response to stabilize the neck. A scoop stretcher, spine board, or vacuum mattress can be used if the patient needs to be transported; however, the patient should not perform any extra movement, such as moving the neck. Overall, it is best to wait for first responders to assist with treatment for this type of situation, but it is important to know how a possible spinal cord injury should be handled.

stem: In which of the following situations is it appropriate for the athletic trainer to use taping techniques? Select all that apply.
choices: A gymnast has foot pain from repetitive landing with flat arches.
A basketball player lands on another player’s foot after a layup and sprains an ankle.
A track athlete sustains a puncture wound to the palm of their hand.
A cross-country athlete falls, resulting in a fractured toe.
A volleyball player sprains their thumb during a match.
rationale: Taping techniques are commonly used to provide compression and support to injured areas or to reduce the risk of re-injury in recovering areas. Specific taping techniques can be used to provide arch support, to stabilize an ankle sprain, to provide support in specific fracture scenarios (such as a fractured toe), or to protect a sprained thumb; however, taping techniques should never be used over an open wound, as would be the case with a puncture wound on the palm.

stem: A patient with a recently torn ACL and meniscus is receiving care from the athletic trainer. What can the athletic trainer do to minimize muscle atrophy as the patient’s injury heals? Select all that apply.
choices: Apply cold therapy at regular intervals.
Immobilize the patient’s leg in a neutral position.
Provide analgesics based on the patient’s level of pain.
Apply manual muscle testing to the quadriceps muscles.
Incorporate isometric muscle contractions into rehabilitation.
rationale: Minimizing muscle atrophy (loss of muscle mass) can be done through immobilizing the muscle in a neutral or lengthened position (rather than a shortened position) and through isometric muscle contractions, where the muscle contracts but does not change length. Cold therapy and analgesics may be used as part of the patient’s post-injury care but would not directly affect muscle atrophy. An athletic trainer would use manual muscle testing to evaluate an acute injury, not as an atrophy reduction technique.

stem: A tennis player experiences a moderate hamstring strain. A week later, he is ready to begin rehabilitation. Which of the following is the most appropriate suggestion for the first week in rehabilitation? Select all that apply.
choices: Rest, Ice, Compression, and Elevation (RICE) protocol
Running on the treadmill for 20 minutes at a time
Light sports massage to the hamstring muscle
rationale: Initial treatment of a moderate hamstring strain might include resting and icing the injured area, along with analgesics such as acetaminophen (Tylenol), to relieve pain. After a week, however, the patient should begin rehabilitation. Static stretches, like the hamstring stretch, are most appropriate. Strengthening the hamstring muscle should also commence, and this can be accomplished using a resistance band. Range of motion exercises that gently begin to work the injured muscle— such as light jogging, stationary bike, or water activities—can gradually begin. These activities should only last a few minutes with a goal of building endurance. Running or excessive use of the injured hamstring should wait until there is no pain with previous exercises. After gentle exercise, ice should be reapplied.

stem: During soccer practice, a player falls against the goal post and sustains a large, bleeding laceration on his arm. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate treatment of the injury?
choices: Either you or the injured player applies a clean bandage and presses the wound while someone else retrieves the first-aid kit and gloves.
Apply a tourniquet above the laceration.
Have the goalie apply pressure to the wound using a clean cloth while retrieving a pair of gloves.
Apply pressure on the femoral artery.
Apply a splint to the arm to help control the bleeding.
rationale: At any sign of blood, universal precautions should be the first step in treatment. Universal precautions will help to stop the spread of bloodborne infection. If the player is conscious and able to apply pressure to the wound on his own, he should be given a sterile or clean bandage and begin to apply pressure while the athletic trainer puts on gloves. If he is not able to do this on his own and the athletic trainer is not able to put on gloves right away, the trainer must make sure to use enough bandages to prevent blood from saturating the bandage and to prevent direct contact with blood. A tourniquet is a last-ditch effort to stop bleeding and is not routinely indicated. Pressure on the femoral artery will not help stop the bleeding for an arm injury because the femoral artery is located in the groin area. A splint is required in the case of a broken bone to help immobilize the area as well as to help stop the bleeding.

stem: An athlete who is trying to gain weight should consume how many additional calories per day?
rationale: Many sports, such as football, basketball, and rugby, require or encourage athletes to gain weight in order to maximize performance. Weight gain must be done gradually. If accomplished too quickly, the resultant weight gain will be in the form of adipose tissue rather than lean body mass. Lean body mass consists of muscle tissue, bones, and connective tissue. An increase in body fat will negatively affect performance. In order for the body to gain weight, an additional 500 calories per day must be consumed above what is expended. A rate of weight gain of approximately 1.5% per week is a good goal. This increase in caloric intake should be coupled with a weight-training program to help build lean body mass.

stem: Which of the following regulatory agencies requires athletic trainers working with professional sports teams to document any injury received by a player?
choices: Commission on Accreditation of Rehabilitation Facilities (CARF)
rationale: Documentation is an important part of an athletic trainer’s job for many reasons. In professional sports, the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) requires athletic trainers to document any injuries received by players. If the athletic trainer works for a facility that is associated with a hospital, clinic, or rehabilitation hospital, requirements for documentation may be set by the Joint Commission (formerly JCAHO) or Commission on Accreditation of Rehabilitation Facilities (CARF). Documentation is important because it provides legal protection, serving as written evidence that care was provided. Documentation also helps the athletic trainer to remember what treatments have been performed with a player and also helps improve communication with other professionals involved in the patient’s care. In some cases, documentation is part of insurance reimbursement.

stem: In which of the following scenarios would an athletic trainer be acting outside the scope of practice? Select all that apply.
choices: The athletic trainer provides general nutrition education to a patient who has been diagnosed with anemia.
The athletic trainer diagnoses a musculoskeletal injury based on what they observe and palpate.
The athletic trainer provides a physician referral to a patient based on abnormal lab test results.
The athletic trainer provides mental health counseling to a patient experiencing performance anxiety.
The athletic trainer performs arthroscopic procedures on a patient with an injured knee.
rationale: The scope of practice for an athletic trainer is based on practice standards, such as implementing measures to prevent injury or illness, using examination procedures for diagnosis, and providing treatment in accordance with their training. The duties within an athletic trainer’s scope of practice can overlap with those of other health professionals; however, it would not be within scope of practice to provide counseling to a patient, as that would require credentialing in mental health, such as that of a counselor, therapist, or psychologist. Arthroscopic procedures would also be out of the scope of practice, as this is a surgical intervention.

stem: A cheerleader falls during a competition. Upon examination, her tibia bone is protruding. What are appropriate steps to take in the immediate treatment of her fracture? Select all that apply.
choices: Move her to a safe place.
Have her lay flat and cover her with a blanket.
Apply sterile dressing to the wound.
rationale: Emergency medical services should be called immediately because of the protruding bone. After making sure the patient is breathing appropriately, initial treatment should begin. The point at which the tibia punctured the skin should be covered with a sterile dressing to prevent infection. The leg should be appropriately immobilized to prevent additional damage to nerves or circulation. Ice can be applied to help prevent swelling. The patient should lie flat and be kept warm using a blanket to help prevent shock. Administration of pain relievers should be delayed until additional medical help arrives, due to the potential for surgery due to the open fracture.

stem: An athletic trainer uses the Trendelenburg’s test on a patient with pain in the left hip and observes a positive test on the right side. What muscle is likely weak in this patient?
rationale: In the Trendelenburg’s test, the patient stands on one leg, lifting one foot to bring the hip into flexion. A positive Trendelenburg’s test is seen when the pelvis drops on the unaffected side. This indicates that the hip abductors, such as the gluteus medius, are lacking the strength and stability to hold the pelvis level. The other four listed choices do not act on the hip in abduction and would not be implicated by a positive Trendelenburg’s test.

stem: An athletic trainer is starting a new role working with patients in their early teens. Which conditions should the athletic trainer be aware of that occur mostly in a physically immature population? Select all that apply.
rationale: Sever’s disease (calcaneal apophysitis) is more common in young athletes and occurs at the bone attachment site of the Achilles tendon. Epiphyseal growth plate injuries have various sub-types and are unique to young athletes whose bones are not yet physically mature. Osgood-Schlatter disease is a type of apophysitis with pain where the patellar tendon attaches to the tibia. Neither cervical dislocations nor syndesmotic ankle sprains are specific to a physically immature population and can be caused by acute trauma at any age.

stem: Which of the following joint mobilization techniques would be most helpful as a therapeutic intervention for de Quervain’s syndrome?
rationale: De Quervain’s syndrome is a type of tenosynovitis in the thumb that results in pain, tenderness, and weakness. The carpometacarpal joint is the saddle joint of the thumb, where anterior/posterior glide mobilizations can help with range of motion. The other listed options are for other areas of the body and not applicable to the thumb.

stem: A patient with a grade 3 inversion ankle sprain has been cleared by the athletic trainer to progress from non-weight-bearing to partial weight-bearing activity. Which of the following exercises or movements would be the most logical choice for her progression?
rationale: Seated balance board exercises would be the most logical progression because they allow the affected ankle to bear partial weight. Jogging, single-leg balance, and shuffle slides are all examples of full weight- bearing activities, and progressing to these too soon could delay the injury’s healing. Anterior tibial glides are a manual mobilization technique used to promote dorsiflexion and are not partial weight-bearing activities.

stem: The athletic trainer for a high school football team is giving an informational presentation on concussions in high school football to the players and parents. Which of the following points should be included in this presentation? Select all that apply.
choices: Symptoms of a concussion can include headache, dizziness, irritability, and a reduced reaction time.
It is imperative that a concussion be identified and treated to allow time for the brain to heal.
It’s acceptable to wait 24 hours before reporting the onset of fuzzy vision and light sensitivity after receiving an elbow to the head during practice.
A patient is typically able to return to playing football within 24 hours of a concussion.
There is a risk of additional brain damage or death if a concussion does not heal sufficiently and another concussion occurs during this time.
rationale: A concussion is a brain injury that occurs in response to a blow to the head. This can happen when a player’s head has direct contact with another player, a hard surface, or a piece of equipment. Concussions can be extremely dangerous and, in some cases, can result in permanent brain damage or death. Each patient will experience a concussion in a different way. Loss of consciousness does not need to occur with a concussion. In football, it is important to discourage any head or helmet contact between players. This includes stepping on another player’s head, elbowing to the head, or any type of hitting. Symptoms of a concussion are wide-ranging but can include confusion, disorientation, vision trouble, headache, nausea, reduced reaction time, and imbalance. It is extremely important for a patient to not ignore these symptoms. The brain needs time to rest and heal. Additional injuries are likely if a concussion does not heal.

stem: A patient with a suspected shoulder injury is being evaluated by the athletic trainer. Which test(s) could be used to assess if the glenohumeral joint lacks stability? Select all that apply.
rationale: Tests used to assess glenohumeral joint (shoulder) instability include the anterior drawer test, the jerk test, and the sulcus test. A positive jerk test indicates posterior shoulder instability due to a labral tear (this is different from the jerk test of Hughston, which is used to assess ACL instability). A positive jerk test indicates posterior shoulder instability due to a labral tear. A positive sulcus test indicates that the glenohumeral and coracohumeral ligament lack stability. The pinch grip test is used for assessing nerve entrapment by the pronator teres. The bowstring test is used to assess irritation from the sciatic nerve.

stem: An athletic trainer is designing a therapeutic program that includes improving flexibility. Which of the following body structures would be potentially targeted in the program? Select all that apply.
rationale: Ligaments, joint capsules, tendons, and muscles (including their surrounding fascia) can all limit flexibility, and therefore may be targeted in a therapeutic program aimed at improving flexibility. Bony structures can limit flexibility, whether in natural movement due to normal end ranges (such as the olecranon process that limits elbow extension) or in calcium deposits from injury; however, a therapeutic program would not target bones, since they do not have elastic properties that would improve through flexibility enhancement techniques.

stem: An athletic trainer was treating a patient who had a cough and a runny nose. Which of the following actions would be effective in avoiding catching or spreading the patient's infection? Select all that apply.
choices: Placing any disposable materials touched by the patient into a biohazard bag
Washing hands immediately with antimicrobial soap and water after treating the patient
Ensuring the patient’s vaccinations are up to date
Using sterile equipment to treat the patient
Having the patient avoid contact with other teammates until symptoms resolve
rationale: Proper handwashing and patient isolation are the most effective preventive measures. A biohazard bag is likely not necessary if there is no danger of coming in contact with the patient’s blood. Vaccinations are important but do not protect against the spread of every type of disease, so universal precautions are still necessary. Sterile equipment is also important but does not address how to prevent the patient’s own pathogens from infecting others.

stem: An athletic trainer can use the HOPS format as a method for evaluating injury. Which components are part of the HOPS format? Select all that apply.
rationale: The HOPS format includes four categories used to evaluate an injury or illness: gathering a detailed history, making general observations, using systematic palpation on bony and soft tissue areas, and performing special tests. Objective findings and plan of care are parts of SOAP (subjective, objective, assessment, plan) notes, not HOPS. Symptom analysis is not a part of the HOPS format.

stem: In which of the following situations is the specified therapeutic modality contraindicated?
choices: A patient experiencing joint stiffness after a muscle strain applies a heat pack to the affected area.
Traction is used on a patient with spondylolisthesis.
A patient with a laceration uses a whirlpool for pain relief.
Compression is used on a patient with a swollen ankle from a grade 3 sprain.
A patient with chronic shoulder pain and limited scapular mobility receives a massage.
rationale: The contraindicated situation is a patient with a laceration using a whirlpool. Using a whirlpool with an open wound is not advised, as it can increase the risk of infection and introduce contamination into the whirlpool.

stem: When measuring a patient’s agility during rehabilitation for a knee injury, which of the following tests might be used? Select all that apply.
rationale: Agility is defined as the ability to start and stop as well as to change directions. Agility in sports is important, and regaining agility following an injury is a goal of rehabilitation. Testing for agility requires a patient to accelerate and decelerate quickly, which can be difficult when recovering from a knee or ankle injury. Results of agility testing should be compared with other patients in similar situations. One test for agility is the Edgren Side Step Test. This test measures how well a patient can sidestep in 3-foot increments in a 12-foot area and then change direction and sidestep back to the other side. The other test for agility is the T- test. This test utilizes cones set up in a T-formation. The patient starts at the first cone and runs forward 10 yards to the second. The patient then runs left 5 yards to the third cone, runs right 10 yards to the fourth cone, runs back to the center, and then back steps to the first cone.

stem: What piece of equipment is most likely to be carried by an athletic trainer to be used in conjunction with cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR)?
rationale: The ability to provide emergency care during sporting events is essential. The certified athletic trainer is able to handle some basic care until first responders arrive on the scene. The pocket mask is the piece of equipment most likely to be carried by an athletic trainer to be used with CPR. This device is used to assist with delivering oxygen to a patient who requires resuscitation. The mask is placed over the patient’s mouth to act as a shield during resuscitation. Oxygen, if available, can also be delivered. Emergency care providers such as emergency medical technicians (EMTs), paramedics, or physicians will have additional equipment such as tubes to open up the airway, bag-valve mask resuscitators, and oxygen tanks. It is imperative that an athletic trainer provide only the level of emergency care for which they have been trained and to let emergency responders take over when they arrive on the scene.

stem: A patient who sustained an injury 4 days ago is being evaluated by the athletic trainer to assess the healing process and determine what therapeutic interventions are appropriate. Which of the following would indicate that the patient is in the inflammatory response phase? Select all that apply.
rationale: Swelling, redness, pain, and heat are all characteristics of acute tissue response to injury that would persist in the inflammatory response phase. The athletic trainer could use this information to determine that therapeutic interventions should focus primarily on inflammation management strategies, such as reducing swelling or controlling pain. Numbness is not a characteristic of the inflammatory response phase.

stem: Which of the following conditions of the head, face, and associated structures would benefit from the use of heat as a therapeutic intervention?
rationale: A stye is an infected or clogged oil gland of the eyelid. Warm, moist compresses are beneficial for styes because they can encourage loosening of the infected or clogged material. Otitis externa (swimmer’s ear) may require antibiotic drops, not heat. A hyphema is an injury caused by a blunt blow to the eye and requires immediate care from an eye professional, though an eye patch may be used in the meantime. Epistaxis is a nosebleed where ice and pressure, rather than heat, would be appropriate interventions. A subdural hematoma is a life-threatening medical emergency, as it can cause hemorrhaging in the brain.

stem: A 60-year-old woman is experiencing mild pain and tenderness in her wrist but is able to move it through full range of motion. She reports no obvious trauma to the area. She has a history of diabetes, controlled with diet and medication. She has fractured her wrist twice in the past 10 years. What are the potential diagnoses based on this clinical evaluation? Select all that apply.
rationale: There are many possible causes of wrist pain. It is unlikely to be due to a fracture or dislocation because this would result in a higher level of pain and inability to move through a full range of motion. Osteoarthritis is a possibility, given her age and history of wrist injuries. Tendonitis and carpal tunnel syndrome are also possible. These types of injuries are typically caused by repetitive motion that uses the wrist for a long period of time without a break, such as playing tennis, using a computer, driving cross country, or playing a string instrument. Additional information should be obtained about recent activities. Treatment can vary based on actual diagnosis but involves pain relievers such as acetaminophen or ibuprofen.

stem: Three athletes on a track and field team have self-reported that they have asthma according to their answers on a medical questionnaire. Which of the following diagnostic instruments could be used to further evaluate the extent of their asthma? Select all that apply.
rationale: Spirometry and a peak flow meter can be used to evaluate the extent of a patient’s asthma. Spirometry measures lung function through forced exhalation, and a peak flow meter measures peak expiratory flow rate. Lower-than-normal numbers in both of these may indicate breathing restrictions. The other options would not be used to evaluate asthma. A complete blood count would be best suited for anemia or infection, echocardiography is used to view valves of the heart, and a glucometer is used to determine levels of blood glucose (to evaluate metabolic syndrome or diabetes).

stem: A cross-country athlete is experiencing tachycardia after consuming a supplement. Which supplement could be responsible for this symptom? Select all that apply.
rationale: Caffeine and ephedrine are central nervous system stimulants that can cause increased heart rate, or tachycardia. Consuming a glucose supplement or creatine in excess could cause gastrointestinal symptoms but not tachycardia. Consuming B-vitamins would not cause tachycardia.

stem: Which of the following would be appropriate to include in a team’s pre-game meal? Select all that apply.
rationale: Pre-game meals should maximize muscle carbohydrate storage and reduce the probability of gastrointestinal distress through delayed stomach emptying. Foods that are easily digested with low-to- medium glycemic indices, such as white rice or toast, are appropriate choices. A liquid food supplement can also be used to provide calories without adding food mass to the stomach that then would need to be digested. In general, foods that are digested relatively slowly or promote gas formation should be avoided. These types of foods include bacon because of its high fat content and soda because of carbonation.

stem: A golfer is experiencing sciatic pain and has received a diagnosis of piriformis syndrome. Which exercise would be most beneficial to improve his condition?
rationale: Strengthening hip abduction is one focus of therapeutic intervention for piriformis syndrome. This could be accomplished through single-leg abduction with tubing. The other movements are either lower body movements or core movements that do not specifically target the hip abductors.

stem: The Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) mandates that any employee who is at risk for exposure to bloodborne pathogens be offered a vaccination for which of the following?
rationale: The Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) mandates that hepatitis B vaccination must be offered to any employee who is at risk of being exposed to bloodborne pathogens. Hepatitis B is a virus that is spread through direct contact with bodily fluids, such as blood from an infected individual. Individuals who are at high risk of contracting the hepatitis B virus include health care workers, athletic trainers, and any workers who have the potential to be exposed to blood. Additionally, OSHA requires that a plan be in place that outlines the procedure for dealing with exposures, including training, staff responsibilities, proper documentation, and the use and availability of personal protective equipment. Any potential biohazardous waste needs to be disposed of in proper containers lined with special red biohazard bags.

stem: A basketball player who weighs 250 pounds (114 kg) is eating a regular, balanced diet. He is trying to build muscle and has started supplementing his diet with a commercial protein shake. What is a good range of daily total protein intake for this patient?
rationale: Protein is an extremely important nutrient, especially for athletes. Protein helps to build and repair muscle tissue and plays a role in many other functions within the body. A normal person requires approximately 0.8 grams per kilogram of protein per day. An athlete has a slightly higher protein requirement of approximately 1.2–1.7 grams per kilogram per day. For the basketball player described in the question, this would be approximately 135–195 grams per day. The majority of athletes can meet their protein requirements through a normal diet. There is no scientifically sound evidence showing that extremely high levels of protein will help build muscle faster. Excessive amounts of protein in the diet will make the kidneys have to work harder to metabolize the protein and may cause the body to lose water in this process. Too much protein can also impact the bones. The athlete’s normal diet should be evaluated by a dietitian to determine if additional protein is required.

stem: Which of the following are appropriate guidelines for recording information in a patient’s medical record? Select all that apply.
choices: Date all entries and legibly sign the entries with a name and title.
Store medical records in a secure place, such as a locked file cabinet.
Erase or white-out any mistakes in documentation.
Write entries in the chronological order that the care occurred.
rationale: The medical record is considered a legal document, and care must be taken when entering information. Only permanent ink should be used, never pencil or erasable ink. Mistakes should never be erased, and entries should not be altered in any way. Each entry should have the time, date, signature, and title of the person providing the documentation. Only factual and objective information can be entered. The care should be written in chronological order. The patient’s response to treatment should be documented. Appropriate and approved medical terminology should be used. Any change in condition, including progress, should be documented. Records should be securely stored so that access is restricted to protect privacy.

stem: An athlete sustains a Grade 2 quadriceps contusion. Which of the following would be contraindicated in initial management of this injury?
choices: Protecting the injury by placing the knee in flexion
Providing compression with an elastic bandage
rationale: Massaging the affected area is the only contraindicated choice out of the listed selections. All other listed options are appropriate in managing the injury. Massaging a quadriceps contusion should be avoided because it can further irritate the injured muscle tissue. With enough irritation, myositis ossificans, which are calcified deposits in the injured muscle, can develop.

stem: Which of the following would be a potential contributor to the development of a heat-related illness during exercise? Select all that apply.
rationale: There are many factors that increase the chance of developing a heat-related illness while exercising. These factors include being older than 65 years or a very young child. Obesity is a risk factor, as is the presence of a medical condition that may further stress the body. The use of alcohol is a risk factor because technique are observed from the frontal and side views with 17 total scored items. The tuck jump test involves 10 seconds of alcohol causes dehydration. Wearing the wrong type of clothing is a risk factor. Hats or other types of headwear are not recommended during exercise because this prevents heat from escaping. Loosely-fitting, lightweight clothing should be selected. Frequent water breaks are recommended to keep athletes hydrated. In addition, athletes from different climates should also become acclimated to a new climate prior to exercise to prevent heat-related illness.

stem: Which of the following is an example of an athletic trainer using evidence-based practice? Select all that apply.
choices: An athletic trainer implements findings from Google Scholar to promote self-myofascial release for track and field athletes.
An athletic trainer asks other local athletic trainers about best practices in a collegiate gymnastics program.
An athletic trainer creates a detailed emergency response plan that outlines the roles of responders and a map of the athletic building.
An athletic trainer completes her emergency cardiac care certificate as part of continuing education.
An athletic trainer changes some of the rehabilitation exercises he uses based on results of multiple randomized controlled trials.
rationale: Evidence-based practice for an athletic trainer involves using the best current data from scientific literature to make decisions. Databases such as Google Scholar can be used to obtain scientific evidence for evaluation. Such scientific evidence, such as that from research studies (including randomized controlled trials), can be used and applied to provide the best possible outcomes for patients. Asking other athletic trainers does not use scientific literature, so it is not considered evidence-based practice. Creation of the emergency response plan is also not part of evidence-based practice. Maintaining the appropriate requirements for continuing education is a responsibility of the athletic trainer but is not an example of using evidence-based practice.

stem: What are the best rehabilitation exercises after a back injury? Select all that apply.
rationale: It is important to begin rehabilitation as soon as possible following a back injury to hasten the healing process. Resuming cardiovascular exercise is an important step. Walking is one of the best exercises to start with because it keeps the spine straight. The patient can walk for a certain number of minutes, based on tolerance, and gradually build up from there. Flexibility or stretching exercises of the hip and leg muscles are also important. If the patient is able to maintain or improve flexibility, it will allow less movement of the spine. Strengthening exercises for the core muscles of the back and abdomen are also indicated. The patient should be advised to work on pulling their belly button inwards towards the spine and hold the muscles tight while remembering to breathe.

stem: An athletic trainer wants to provide a patient with non-weighted, open kinetic chain exercises that he can implement at home as part of his rehabilitation process for strengthening an injured knee. Which exercise would suit this purpose?
choices: Straight leg raises on stability ball
rationale: Following knee injury, there are a number of exercises that can be done to strengthen the knee without the use of equipment. In an open kinetic chain exercise for the lower body, the distal part of the leg moves freely in space without being affixed to the ground, such as in a side-lying abduction. While leg extensions are also open kinetic chain, isokinetic leg extensions are done on a specialized machine and would not be an example of an unweighted exercise that could be done at home. Lateral lunges, single-leg deadlifts, and mini squats are all closed kinetic chain exercises where the distal segment (the feet) are in contact with the ground. While straight leg raises on a stability ball are open kinetic chain, this is a core stabilization exercise and would not specifically target strengthening an injured knee.

stem: In which situation would the athletic trainer need to move or reposition an unresponsive patient before performing CPR? Select all that apply.
choices: A high school athlete who is laying supine on a gym floor
An adult who is outdoors during audible thunder
A child who is in a swimming pool
An older adult who is lying prone
A college student who goes into shock after vomiting and is laying on their side
rationale: CPR can only be performed when a patient is lying supine (on their back) and when the environment is safe. A patient outdoors during a thunderstorm is in immediate danger of lightning strike and will need to be moved for their safety and the rescuer’s safety. A patient in a pool must be removed from the water before administering CPR and will need to be dried off if an automated external defibrillator (AED) is used. A patient lying prone or on their side will not be in the proper position for the chest compressions and breaths required for CPR. Careful repositioning is necessary in case the person has other injuries that could be made worse with movement, such as a cervical spine injury.

stem: A volleyball athlete comes to the athletic trainer with complaints of gradually worsening, dull, aching knee pain after practices and games that goes away with rest. Observation of the knee shows no swelling. The athlete most likely has:
rationale: Based on the patient’s reported symptoms and observed signs, the patient most likely has patellar tendinopathy, also known as “jumper’s knee.” This is a chronic inflammatory condition that typically occurs with overuse. The other patellar conditions listed would present with swelling and would typically occur from acute causes, such as trauma or doing a cutting maneuver.

stem: Which of the following injuries or conditions is likely to be improved by use of a knee brace? Select all that apply.
rationale: Providing stabilization for the knee by using a brace can benefit patellofemoral pain syndrome, post-surgical healing of knee ligament injuries, and patellar dislocations. A knee plica occurs from a fibrotic synovial fold, where inflammation is best minimized through rest, heat, and analgesics rather than using a knee brace. A femoral neck fracture occurs in the hip, not the knee, so a knee brace would not be used.

stem: An athletic trainer wants to conduct testing for athletes on the women’s soccer team to help reduce the occurrence of ACL injuries. Which test would be most helpful for this purpose? Select all that apply.
rationale: Both the LESS test and the tuck jump test can be used to identify athletes who are at higher risk of ACL injuries. The LESS test involves a jump-landing movement with rebounding, where errors in landingrepeated tuck jumps with visual observation of deficits based on 6 common mistakes. Athletes demonstrating poor form on either or both tests are candidates for targeted injury prevention strategies. The other three listed tests are not applicable to ACL injury prevention. The T-test measures agility, the sit-and- reach test measures hamstring and low back flexibility, and the stork test measures balance.

stem: A 60-year-old man with cardiovascular disease has been medically cleared to begin an exercise program. What test would be an appropriate measure of cardiovascular endurance? Select all that apply.
rationale: The main purpose of fitness testing is to determine a baseline level of fitness. This can help designate the most appropriate exercises to perform. The tests used to test cardiovascular endurance can depend on the age or health status of the patient being tested. For a 60-year-old man with heart disease, the Rockport Fitness Walking test and Harvard Step Test are the best choices. The Rockport Fitness Walking test requires the participant to walk one mile as quickly as possible. At the end of this walk, the participant's heart rate is taken, and his maximal volume of oxygen uptake (VO2 max) is calculated. Changes in this value over time are used to assess progress. The Harvard Step Test involves the use of steps that are 20 inches high. The participant steps up and down at a rate of 30 steps per minute until reaching exhaustion, which is defined as the point at which the participant is not able to keep the pace for 15 seconds. At this point, the heart rate is monitored at one-, two-, and three-minute intervals. The results are converted to the number of heartbeats in the recovery period and are evaluated on a range of poor to excellent based on score.

stem: Which of the following statements is true regarding the initial treatment of a strain or sprain? Select all that apply.
choices: A patient with a strain or sprain should go immediately to the emergency room or call 911.
A patient with a sprain should wear a splint, cast, or elastic bandages to reduce swelling.
A patient with a sprain or a strain should go to a physician for further evaluation.
A patient with a strain or sprain should try to walk it off and reassess it in a couple hours.
A patient with a strain or sprain should take pain medication such as acetaminophen.
rationale: If a patient might have a strain or a sprain, they should be advised to refrain from using the affected part of the body. A physician can better assess the injury, and an x-ray may be required. Referral to an emergency room or a call to emergency medical services is not usually necessary, although some patients may choose to be seen in an urgent care facility. A sprained area will most likely require some type of support, such as a splint, cast, or elastic bandage. An area with a strain will require rest. Both types of injuries will benefit from some type of pain relief medication, such as acetaminophen. Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), such as ibuprofen, are not recommended because they can interfere with the body's inflammatory process, which is important for healing. A strain will usually heal within a week, whereas a sprain may take as long as four weeks to heal.

stem: Which of the following upper body conditions would commonly be treated with ultrasound? Select all that apply.
choices: Muscle spasms in the spinal erectors
rationale: Ultrasound is typically used for reducing pain and inflammation and stimulating repair of soft tissue injuries through deep heating. Adhesive capsulitis (“frozen shoulder”), muscle spasms, and shoulder impingement can all benefit from ultrasound treatment. In thoracic outlet compression, nerves are compressed due to various anatomical conditions. This would be treated with stretching and strengthening rather than ultrasound. Ultrasound also would not typically be used on a broken bone that is healing, such as a clavicular fracture.

stem: A patient is recovering from a fractured clavicle. He no longer requires the use of a sling and is beginning rehabilitation. Which of the following exercises are appropriate to begin strengthening the clavicle? Select all that apply.
choices: Bicep curls and triceps extensions with weights
Bicep curls and triceps extensions without weights
Internal and external rotation using exercise bands
rationale: A fractured clavicle or collarbone can take up to 12 weeks to fully heal, depending upon the age of the patient. When entering the rehabilitation phase, the main goals are to maximize flexibility in the shoulder, improve range of motion, and strengthen the muscles surrounding the collarbone area, including the shoulders, upper arms, upper back, and chest. After initial treatment with immobilization using a sling, the patient is gradually able to increase the level of activity in the shoulder. Initially, Codman’s exercises are utilized, which consist of bending at the waist and allowing the arms to move like a pendulum. Next, the patient can progress to additional strengthening exercises if no pain is present. These exercises are biceps curls, shoulder shrugs, triceps extensions, and arm raises done without the use of weights. As healing progresses, the use of elastic bands followed by weights can be incorporated. Returning to full activity requires verification by x-ray that the fracture is completely healed.

stem: What is the difference between a sprain and a strain? Select all that apply.
choices: There is no real difference between the terms.
A strain hurts more than a sprain.
A strain is pain in the muscle, and a sprain is overstretched or torn ligaments.
A sprain is common in areas like the ankle, knee, or wrist, whereas a strain is common in the back, legs, or neck.
A strain will not show any bruising.
rationale: A sprain and a strain are similar injuries, but there are some differences. They are similar because both injuries cause pain and may cause some swelling and bruising. A sprain can occur when the ligaments have been overstretched or torn, causing a mild to severe sprain. A sprain will typically cause pain immediately and the patient may not be able to put any pressure on the injured area at all. Sprains will usually involve wrists, knees or ankles. A strain is pain in a muscle that has been stretched too far. The most common areas for strains are the back, legs, or neck. Strains are more likely to occur if a patient does not properly warm up before engaging in certain activities.

stem: Which of the following is contraindicated when initiating treatment for cold stress or cold injury?
choices: Use a heating pad or warm water bath for gradual rewarming.
Provide massage to the affected area.
Palpate the area to discern the presence of frostbite.
Provide warm fluids for the individual to drink.
rationale: Cold injury is most likely to occur in the hands, feet, nose, and ears. Cold injury can cause extensive tissue damage if not treated appropriately. To treat cold injury, wet clothing should be removed, and the affected area should be gradually rewarmed, such as through heating pads or warm water. Palpation may be used to discern if the area is hard or numb, such as in frostbite. Warm fluids can be provided as they help increase core temperature; however, the area should not be massaged as this may cause tissue damage or a further decrease in core temperature.

stem: While lifting weights, an athlete experiences lower back pain on one side of her body. What tests should the athletic trainer perform to assess for lumbar disc herniation? Select all that apply.
rationale: The athletic trainer is not qualified to do many medical tests such as x-ray or MRI. If needed, these types of tests will most likely be done by a licensed physician or medical professional. Though electrostimulation as a treatment is within the scope of practice for an athletic trainer, it would not be performed as a test. The athletic trainer can do a couple of simple tests during a physical examination to try to assess the lower back pain. The first is the straight leg raise (SLR) test. The patient should lie flat on her back and try to lift the leg straight up. The knee should remain straight. If pain is felt down the leg and below the knee, a herniated disc is very likely. A second test to assess weakness is the heel-toe walk. Inability to do this type of walk may signal nerve compression in the spine, caused by a herniated disc. A referral to a physician is needed in this case. Treatment typically involves rest and anti-inflammatory medication for pain relief. Sometimes, physical therapy or steroid injections may be recommended.

stem: Which of the following are required of athletic trainers? Select all that apply.
choices: Following Board of Certification (BOC) practice standards
Following Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) requirements
Following Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA) requirements
Graduating from an accredited program with a minimum of an associate’s degree in athletic training
rationale: To become an athletic trainer, an individual must graduate with a bachelor’s degree from an accredited institution. Next, the individual must pass the examination offered by the Board of Certification (BOC) to become certified in athletic training (ATC). In order to work in most states, the individual must apply and meet the requirements for registration, licensure, or certification. This is state-based and not a federal requirement or law. Athletic trainers are subject to the rules of the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) and, if employed in a school, the Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA). FERPA is a law that deals with privacy of student education records; in most cases, health records kept by school employees are considered to be part of the education record and therefore fall under FERPA regulations rather than HIPAA. Athletic trainers are also required to follow the BOC practice standards and code of ethics. In order to become recertified, continuing education guidelines must be followed.

stem: A football athlete recovering from injury has progressed through many components of his rehabilitation and is now working on incorporating functional, sport-specific progressions, like decelerating and changing direction. What movements would be most appropriate to include in this athlete’s warm-up for such a program? Select all that apply.
rationale: The purpose of a warm-up is to prepare the body for activity through increasing blood flow to muscles and enhancing the role of the nervous system, such as through promoting coordination.
