Skipping with low knees, forward lunges, single-leg abductions with tubing, and dynamic stretches would all be activity- specific preparations for the working joints and muscles used in actions like decelerating and changing direction. Ballistic stretching has concerns for injury with applying repetitive, jerking force on muscle tissue and has not been shown to be an effective part of a warm-up.

stem: Which of the following is an indication that a patient is suffering from a chronic injury instead of an acute injury?
choices: Dull ache when the body is at rest
Inability to perform weight-bearing activity on a lower extremity
Inability to perform full range of motion in the affected extremity
rationale: An acute injury is one that is considered to have occurred recently due to some type of trauma. A chronic injury is one that is due to overuse or due to a condition that has been present for an extended period of time. Symptoms of an acute injury include sudden and severe pain and swelling, tenderness, or extreme weakness. Full range of motion in the affected area may not be possible. There may be an obvious visible break or dislocation of a bone. Weight-bearing activity will likely not be possible with an acute injury. Chronic injuries, on the other hand, typically will result in a dull, achy pain when the body is at rest or not engaged in any particular activity. Swelling may also be present. Pain with either type of injury should not be ignored.

stem: A ball-and-socket joint, such as the shoulder, is capable of which of the following types of motion? Select all that apply.
rationale: A ball-and-socket joint is a type of joint in which a ball moves within a socket so that rotary movement is allowed in almost any direction. Examples of this type of joint are the shoulder and hip. The range of motion seen in ball-and-socket joints are flexion and extension (bending and straightening), abduction and adduction (pulling away from the midline and bringing back), and internal and external rotation. Pronation and supination are seen in the forearm when twisting the palm to face up or down. Dorsiflexion and plantar flexion are seen in the foot when the foot is bent upwards or downwards. Eversion and inversion are another set of terms applying to the foot when the sole is rotated away or toward the median. During rehabilitation, one of the goals is to increase range of motion in ball-and-socket joints in order to return to as close to pre- injury status as possible.

stem: A patient who exhibits over-pronation in her walking and running gait is more likely to experience which of the following? Select all that apply.
rationale: While some degree of pronation is part of normal gait, over-pronation can cause altered foot biomechanics during the stance phase of gait. Over time, this can lead to conditions like plantar fasciitis or Achilles tendonitis. Chronic ankle instability tends to result from recurrent ankle sprains, not from over- pronation. Pes cavus foot is a foot with a higher arch than normal and is usually associated with too much supination, not over-pronation. Compartment syndrome is the result of traumatic or excessive activity and is not directly related to over-pronation.

stem: In which situation would naloxone (Narcan) likely need to be administered? Select all that apply.
choices: A patient has sustained a suspected brain hemorrhage from head trauma and is unresponsive.
A patient prescribed oxycodone for pain has been taking increasingly large amounts and is found unconscious from an overdose.
A patient takes amphetamines to perform better and starts to experience rapid heart rate and convulsions.
A patient went to a party and used heroin. Her friends found her unresponsive shortly after.
A patient was found with hypothermia and frostbite after getting lost on a trail run.
rationale: Naloxone (Narcan) is a medication that temporarily reverses the effects of an opioid overdose. Naloxone is effective for reversal of all opioid overdoses, including those from illicit drugs such as heroin or prescriptions such as oxycodone. Naloxone can be injected or used as a nasal spray. While all of the other incidents are potentially life-threatening and require immediate care, naloxone (Narcan) would not be part of the critical incident response.

stem: Which of the following is considered part of the operating budget for the athletic training room in a high school? Select all that apply.
rationale: A budget is a financial plan that dictates how the athletic training room will function for a certain period of time. An operating budget includes the items needed for the day-to-day operations and is typically written for a one-year period. A capital budget includes items that are required for long-term operations. A capital budget is projected for 5–10 years. The money for these two budgets is separate from each other. Typical items in the operating budget for an athletic training room include first-aid supplies, various instruments needed such as scissors, and products needed for the different modalities employed, such as flexi- wrap, heat packs, and ice bags. All the various types of tape needed would be included in the operating budget. Items for the capital budget would be more expensive items such as a taping bench or whirlpool.

stem: An athletic trainer is obtaining a history from an injured ski team athlete. The patient reports that one of his skis got stuck in a patch of snow. His foot was moving to the right, but his knee moved to the left. The patient reports pain and swelling in the right knee. What is the most likely mechanism of injury?
rationale: Based on the information from the scenario and the injured athlete’s history, shear stress from acute trauma is the most likely mechanism of injury. Shear stress involves opposing forces, as described by the athlete with his foot moving one way while his knee moved the other way. This injury is also most likely an acute trauma based on the cause-and-effect of shear stress leading to pain and swelling in the knee.

stem: What are the reported benefits of massage therapy for rehabilitation following a sports injury? Select all that apply.
choices: Increased nerve excitability in surrounding tissues
rationale: Massage is an example of a manual therapeutic modality that involves pressing and manipulating various parts of the body through the skin, muscles, tendons, and ligaments. The form of massage can vary from light touch to deep pressure techniques. In sports, massage is used to help treat or prevent injury to specific muscles. There are many reported benefits to massage therapy, including relief of spasms or muscle pain, improvement in blood flow (which also promotes healing), and increased cell metabolism. This means that cells are regenerating in the affected area. Other reported benefits include helping to restore full range of motion. Athletic trainers should be suitably trained in massage techniques in order to prevent injuries such as nerve damage, bleeding, and temporary paralysis.

stem: An athletic trainer is designing a program to help a patient strengthen the muscles of his rotator cuff. Which of the following exercises will be most effective?
rationale: The muscles of the rotator cuff (supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, and subscapularis) are a group of muscles that function in abduction, internal rotation, and external rotation and provide stability to the glenohumeral joint. Targeting the rotator cuff muscles is best accomplished through external rotation with resistance bands. While the other movements may recruit the rotator cuff muscles to some extent as stabilizers, these movements will not be as effective at directly targeting the rotator cuff muscles.

stem: Which of the following is an example of a primary survey assessment during a critical incident? Select all that apply.
choices: Gathering a detailed medical history from the patient
Evaluating the extent of a sprain
rationale: Actions taken by the athletic trainer in the primary survey assessment evaluate if life-threatening conditions are present, such as airway obstruction or life-threatening injuries. This assessment helps the athletic trainer determine the urgency of further emergency care for the patient. Medical history, pupil response, and evaluating the extent of a sprain would all be part of the secondary survey rather than the primary survey.

stem: Which of the following athletes would be most likely to develop medial tibial stress syndrome (MTSS)? Select all that apply.
rationale: Medial tibial stress syndrome, often referred to as shin splints, is anterior shin pain caused by repetitive microtrauma, such as from running and jumping. The repetitive jumping and running in basketball and repetitive running in cross country can both cause this microtrauma. Baseball pitching, swimming, and Olympic weightlifting are not athletic activities that typically place repetitive running or jumping impacts on the lower body.

stem: Which of the following are common symptoms of circadian dysrhythmia? Select all that apply.
rationale: Circadian dysrhythmia, also known as jet lag, can occur when an individual travels outside of their normal time zone, resulting in various symptoms of physiological stress such as changes in bowel habits, digestive problems, headache, and fatigue. The other listed symptoms are typically related to exposure to environmental extremes, such as extreme heat, extreme cold, or high altitude.

stem: A collegiate athletic trainer is giving an information presentation to student athletes on the effects of alcohol on athletic training. Which of the following points should be included in this presentation? Select all that apply.
choices: Alcohol provides empty calories at approximately 150–200 calories per drink.
The body converts alcohol to fatty acids, which can lead to weight gain.
Alcohol interferes with the quality of sleep an athlete receives.
The effects of alcohol can last for a few days, leading to a reduction in reaction time and decreased ability to concentrate.
rationale: Alcohol can have a significant and lasting effect on athletic performance. The effects of one night of drinking can last as long as three days and can affect overall performance and reaction time. Alcohol is considered a source of empty calories because it does not provide vitamins or minerals. The body processes alcohol similar to how it processes fat, which can lead to excessive fat deposition and weight gain. Alcohol interferes with the quality of sleep, which can also impact performance and lead to fatigue. Alcohol dehydrates the body; this can be dangerous for athletes because, due to stress and environmental conditions, they require more fluids to keep hydrated. Alcohol can increase the risk of injury while playing a sport and also impacts the body’s ability to recover following an injury. Alcohol can also lead to long-term health issues.

stem: An athletic trainer is designing a therapeutic program that incorporates core stability. Which muscle or muscle group would be part of this core stability program? Select all that apply.
rationale: Enhancing core stability involves targeting the muscles in the lumbo-pelvic-hip complex that act on the lumbar spine, abdominals, and hips, such as the latissimus dorsi, multifidus, and psoas. The trapezius acts on the scapula, the semimembranosus is part of the hamstring muscle group, and the intercostals are the muscles between the ribs; therefore, these would not be specifically implicated in a core stability program.

stem: Over the course of two days in the off-season, a baseball athlete has started to experience redness, irritation, and discomfort in one eye. The athletic trainer should do which of the following? Select all that apply.
choices: Apply intermittent cold and heat to the eye until symptoms improve.
Have the patient avoid contact with others.
Advise the patient to seek treatment from an eye care professional.
Provide the patient with NSAIDs to reduce eye inflammation.
Instruct the patient to wear an eye patch over the affected eye during practices and games.
rationale: The symptoms described in this scenario are likely indicative of conjunctivitis (pink eye), which is usually caused by a viral infection, bacterial infection, or allergic reaction. As some causes of this condition can be highly contagious, the patient should be instructed to avoid contact with others and to seek treatment from an eye care professional to resolve the condition. For conjunctivitis, preventing transmission to others is critical. The other options are not effective infection prevention practices.

stem: A female student patient may have an eating disorder. She has experienced significant weight loss, and a few of her teammates have approached the athletic trainer with concerns regarding her use of laxatives and restrictive eating. The athletic trainer discusses this issue with the coach. What is the most appropriate plan for addressing this situation with the patient?
choices: Do not allow her to participate in training or competition until she receives a full medical examination.
Call her parents and tell them to deal with this issue immediately.
Make a direct referral to an eating disorder specialist or counselor on campus.
Ask one of her teammates to talk to her about the health concerns, and if she is receptive, refer her to a health care specialist.
Privately approach her and express concerns regarding her health, avoiding the topic of eating habits or weight, and arrange a medical examination.
rationale: The most important step for beginning to deal with a student athlete who is suspected of having disordered eating is to make the initial contact non-threatening. This needs to be done by a person of authority, such as the athletic trainer or any of the coaching staff. The patient should be approached in private and should not be made to feel uncomfortable or embarrassed. Discussions about her weight or disordered eating should not be brought up at this time. Instead, the focus should be on her health and well-being. She should be advised that a medical examination is being arranged and that it is not optional. She will not be allowed to participate in the sport until the issue is addressed. A referral to a health care provider specializing in disordered eating may be necessary. This health care professional may also need to talk to the team about the issue, although confidentiality will prevent this person from directly discussing the patient.

stem: A pre-participation assessment for a patient discloses that he has been diagnosed with iron- deficiency anemia. What food(s) will be most helpful in maintaining or improving his performance?
rationale: A patient with iron-deficiency anemia will benefit from consuming foods that are high in iron to maintain or increase iron stores. Red meat, such as beef, is one of the best sources of dietary iron. The other listed foods contain minimal or no iron and will not be helpful for managing iron-deficiency anemia.

stem: A mixed martial arts athlete gets hit in the mouth during a match, dislodging a tooth and causing the athlete to begin choking. If the athlete becomes unresponsive, what should the athletic trainer do next once the athlete is safely lowered to the ground and EMS is contacted?
choices: Check the athlete for a pulse.
Provide abdominal thrusts to dislodge the tooth.
Check the athlete’s mouth for the dislodged tooth.
rationale: An unresponsive person with an obstructed airway, such as occurs when someone is choking, should receive CPR immediately. CPR should start with chest compressions once they are safely lying supine and EMS has been contacted. After 30 chest compressions, the airway should be opened to observe if the object is in the mouth and can be swept out. Providing immediate CPR is more important than checking for a pulse in this situation, and abdominal thrusts are only used for a conscious patient with a partial airway obstruction.

stem: An athlete whose medical history includes severe allergies to wasps has joined the cross-country team. What medication should the athlete and the athletic trainer have on hand?
rationale: Any patient with severe allergies of this nature is at risk for anaphylactic shock if exposed to the allergen. The athlete and the athletic trainer should have epinephrine (in the form of an injectable Epi-Pen) on hand to manage a potential anaphylactic reaction. The other medications listed will not provide immediate help in the event of anaphylaxis.

stem: An athletic trainer observes scapular winging at the medial border in a patient. Which exercise would be the most helpful therapeutic intervention for this patient? Select all that apply.
choices: Shoulder Y-T-W exercises on a physio ball
rationale: Scapular winging at the medial border indicates weakness of the trapezius, rhomboids, and serratus anterior, which are scapular stabilizers. The push-up plus and its many variations strengthen the serratus anterior. Shoulder Y-T-Ws on a physio ball and scapular adduction and downward rotation both promote strengthening of the trapezius and rhomboids. The implicated muscles for winging need to be strengthened rather than stretched, so the sleeper stretch or rhomboid stretch would not be applicable in this situation.

stem: In what situation would an athlete be most likely to contract HBV or HIV? Select all that apply.
choices: An athlete used a hot tub when they had an open abrasion on their skin.
An athlete consumes undercooked meat at a team meal.
An athlete shares needles with others to inject performance-enhancing drugs.
An athlete has multiple sexual partners and rarely uses condoms.
An athlete shares a water bottle with other teammates.
rationale: HBV (hepatitis B virus) and HIV (human immunodeficiency virus) are transmitted by pathogens found in blood and can be transmitted through sharing needles or engaging in risky sexual practices. While other bodily fluids can transmit these pathogens, contracting HBV or HIV through a hot tub, sharing a water bottle, or from contaminated food would be nearly, if not completely, impossible.

stem: An athletic trainer wants to use measurements to assess if a patient recovering from injury is regaining range of motion. Which of the following could the athletic trainer employ to assess range of motion? Select all that apply.
choices: Use of the Functional Movement Screen
rationale: Goniometric measurement and use of an inclinometer can both assess the range of motion at a given joint. The athletic trainer could use either of these to compare the patient’s measurements over time to determine their range of motion recovery. Testing deep tendon reflexes or using anthropometric measurements would not provide information on range of motion. While the Functional Movement Screen includes mobility as part of its seven movements, it tests dynamic functional movements and not exclusively individual joint range of motion.

stem: An athlete performs a seated row on a cable machine. She initiates the movement by contracting the muscles of the rhomboids and latissimus dorsi. What type of movement is this? Select all that apply.
rationale: Isotonic movements involve changes in muscle length while force is being generated. Isotonic movements may have a concentric component, an eccentric component, or both. From the description, this exercise is a concentric isotonic exercise where the rhomboids and latissimus dorsi are shortening (contracting) against the resistance. The scenario does not describe an isometric movement (where muscles contract but do not change in length) or an isokinetic movement (where specialized equipment provides a fixed speed to maintain the same level of resistance throughout the range of motion).

stem: What is the medical term that describes a dangerous increase in pressure within the muscles?
rationale: Compartment syndrome is the medical term used to describe an extremely painful condition that occurs when pressure in the muscles increases to a dangerously high level. Compartment syndrome can be acute or chronic, but an acute case is considered a medical emergency. It can occur with acute trauma to a muscle because the fascia (or thick tissue) that surrounds and separates muscle groups is not able to expand. This increase in pressure or swelling within the fascia stops the muscle and nerve cells from receiving oxygen and nutrients and can lead to permanent nerve damage. Chronic compartment syndrome can be caused by activities that utilize repetitive movements, such as running. The symptoms of this syndrome include loss of sensation to the area, severe pain, and weakness. The skin will appear pale. On physical examination, the patient will experience pain in the area if the muscle compartment is squeezed or attempt is made to move the area.

stem: Which of the following is TRUE regarding the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA)- mandatory medical examination of student athletes?
choices: The medical examination must be conducted by the team physician.
The student athlete may participate in conditioning or limited practices prior to the completion of the medical examination.
The student athlete will require an updated medical examination each year only if their health condition changes.
The medical examination must be conducted within six months prior to participating in any type of conditioning, practice, or competition associated with the team.
The medical examination must be conducted within one month prior to participating in any type of conditioning, practice, or competition associated with the team.
rationale: The National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) mandates that all student athletes undergo a complete medical examination within six months of starting any type of required preseason conditioning, practice, or competition. This mandate includes student athletes who are starting their first season of NCAA eligibility and transfers. This medical examination must be completed prior to the start of the academic year and covers the academic year and summer. Student athletes are then required to receive an updated medical examination each academic year. Appropriate documentation must be submitted and retained for the athletes’ entire college career. The examination may be completed by the team physician or family physician. It may also be completed by a nurse practitioner or physician assistant working under the direction of a licensed medical doctor.

stem: A rugby athlete is elbowed in the eye by an opponent during a match. Which of the following are appropriate steps in responding to this injury? Select all that apply.
choices: Check eye movements and pupil dilation.
Assess if there are related injuries to the face, head, or neck.
Apply padding and elastic wrap to provide pressure on the eye.
Cover both eyes if the athlete is being transported for further emergency care.
Ask the athlete if they are experiencing any loss of vision.
rationale: Responding to an acute eye injury includes assessments of eye movements, pupil dilation, related injuries, or loss of vision. In addition, both eyes should be covered for emergency transport so that the injured eye does not move. Pressure should not be applied to eye injuries.

stem: During a high school varsity football practice, the temperature is 95°F. One of the linebackers is complaining of nausea, lightheadedness, and fatigue. He has a headache and vomited approximately 30 minutes earlier. What should be done to initiate treatment? Select all that apply.
choices: Have him go inside where there is air conditioning.
Provide a pain reliever for the headache.
Provide 4 ounces of water or sports drink every 15 minutes.
Use a fan to help cool the skin, and apply cool compresses on pulse points.
Provide salt tablets to help replace lost electrolytes.
rationale: The student athlete is showing signs of heat exhaustion. Some of the early symptoms are dizziness, lightheadedness, extreme sweating, nausea, muscle cramps, weakness, and fatigue. Late symptoms include headache, nausea, vomiting, and dilated pupils. As heat exhaustion progresses, it can lead to irrational behavior and loss of consciousness. Immediate treatment consists of moving the patient to a cooler place and elevating feet. A fan can be used to help lower body temperature, and cool cloths can be placed on the skin at various pulse points such as the neck, armpits, or groin. The patient should begin to slowly rehydrate with cool water or sports beverages with approximately 4 ounces every 15 minutes. Pain relievers should not be administered. Salt tablets should not be given because these may cause harm. If the patient does not show signs of recovery or begins to show signs of shock or seizure, emergency care should be sought.

stem: In which of the following scenarios could the athletic trainer be held liable for the athlete’s injury? Select all that apply.
choices: A few lacrosse balls were left on the field from the lacrosse team’s practice earlier that day.
The injured player was wearing too-large shoes from another teammate because she had forgotten hers at home.
The soccer field was slick and wet from a rainstorm an hour earlier.
The injured player was still recovering from a mild calf muscle strain, but the athletic trainer allowed her to participate in practice.
Another athlete tripped the injured player because of a disagreement they had prior to practice.
rationale: If an athletic trainer is held liable for harm, this means they had a legal responsibility that was not upheld, even if this was unintentional or accidental. An athletic trainer’s responsibilities include providing an acceptable standard of care, which includes being aware of risks such as equipment or debris that can obstruct play, potential dangers from environmental hazards, or making decisions that put an athlete at higher potential for injury, such as having them return to play prematurely post-injury.
scenario: A group of female high school soccer athletes is playing a practice match when one of the athletes appears to trip before collapsing to the ground. Upon trying to stand, she is unable to place full weight upon her left foot and can walk only with difficulty. Based on an initial assessment, the athletic trainer suspects a grade 2 inversion sprain of the anterior talofibular ligament.

stem: What special test would be most suitable to use for assessment of this patient's particular suspected injury?
rationale: The anterior drawer test can be used to assess the extent of injury if injury to the anterior talofibular ligament is suspected. This test involves pushing the tibia backward while pulling the calcaneus forward. Observation of the foot sliding forward and a clunking sound indicates a positive anterior drawer test. The other tests would be used for other types of suspected lower extremity injuries, such as fracture, Achilles tendon rupture, or meniscal tears.
scenario: A group of female high school soccer athletes is playing a practice match when one of the athletes appears to trip before collapsing to the ground. Upon trying to stand, she is unable to place full weight upon her left foot and can walk only with difficulty. Based on an initial assessment, the athletic trainer suspects a grade 2 inversion sprain of the anterior talofibular ligament.

stem: Which of the following modalities would likely be beneficial for treating this patient? Select all that apply.
rationale: Compression, analgesics (such as Tylenol) to relieve pain, and use of crutches for partial weight- bearing are all beneficial in managing pain and swelling while reducing stress on the injured area. Traction is often used to stretch ligaments and joint capsules. It would not be beneficial for an ankle sprain, ash the affected ligaments have already been stressed and further stretching could cause damage. Dry needling is typically used for trigger points within muscles and would not be a first-line therapeutic intervention for sprained ankle ligaments.
scenario: A group of female high school soccer athletes is playing a practice match when one of the athletes appears to trip before collapsing to the ground. Upon trying to stand, she is unable to place full weight upon her left foot and can walk only with difficulty. Based on an initial assessment, the athletic trainer suspects a grade 2 inversion sprain of the anterior talofibular ligament.

stem: The first stages of a rehabilitation program for this patient should include which of the following? Select all that apply.
choices: Maintaining cardiovascular fitness through non-weight-bearing exercise
Implementing standing exercises on an unstable surface
Doing strengthening exercises with light resistance in varied planes of motion
Use of plantarflexion and dorsiflexion exercises
rationale: The first stages of rehabilitation for this patient need to protect the injured structures as they are healing. Non-weight-bearing cardiorespiratory activities, such as using a stationary bike or ergometer, can help maintain fitness while avoiding stress on the injured area. Moving through pain-free ranges of motion in dorsiflexion and plantar flexion will help the ankle joint stay mobile and ideally regain full function. While the other options would be part of ankle sprain rehabilitation, these would be used in the later stages of rehabilitation once healing has progressed.
scenario: A group of female high school soccer athletes is playing a practice match when one of the athletes appears to trip before collapsing to the ground. Upon trying to stand, she is unable to place full weight upon her left foot and can walk only with difficulty. Based on an initial assessment, the athletic trainer suspects a grade 2 inversion sprain of the anterior talofibular ligament.

stem: Which of the following is an example of the athletic trainer promoting health literacy in this patient? Select all that apply.
choices: Educating the patient on surgery as a treatment option
Using a visual aid to explain the anatomy of the ankle ligaments
Obtaining consent to treat from both the patient and her parents
Providing an illustrated handout that shows range-of-motion exercises the patient can do at home
Using clinical terminology to outline the plans for rehabilitation
rationale: Athletic trainers promote health literacy in those under their care when they provide guidance in making informed decisions based on the ability to understand and use health-related information. A visual aid can help the patient develop an accurate mental picture of her injury and understand the affected structures. An illustrated handout with rehabilitative exercises can empower the patient to understand the active role the patient plays in her own healing. While education on surgery would be an example of health literacy in some cases, a grade 2 ankle sprain does not require surgery; therefore, this is not a correct answer in this situation. Consent is an important aspect of treatment but is not part of health literacy. Using clinical terminology could hinder health literacy rather than promote it because the patient may not understand this terminology.
scenario: A group of female high school soccer athletes is playing a practice match when one of the athletes appears to trip before collapsing to the ground. Upon trying to stand, she is unable to place full weight upon her left foot and can walk only with difficulty. Based on an initial assessment, the athletic trainer suspects a grade 2 inversion sprain of the anterior talofibular ligament.

stem: Which type of therapeutic technique or exercise would best help a patient regain neuromuscular control when recovering from a knee injury? Select all that apply.
choices: Standing exercises on unstable surfaces like a Bosu Balance trainer or rocker board
rationale: Regaining neuromuscular control after an injury involves retraining the central nervous system and the body to work together to consciously control movements. Neuromuscular control may include aspects such as balance and proprioception, which would be enhanced through exercises on unstable surfaces. The recovery program may include performing repetitions of functional strengthening exercises while maintaining proper form, such as multiplanar lunges. Performing such exercises underwater can also improve neuromuscular control. The other options could be used as therapeutic interventions for a knee injury but do not specifically address the objective of regaining neuromuscular control.

stem: A 19-year-old patient with a resting heart rate of 61 asks the athletic trainer how to determine if she is exercising at a vigorous intensity (80% of maximum heart rate). To determine the patient’s maximum heart rate (MHR), use the following equation: 𝐌𝐇𝐑 = 𝟐𝟏𝟏 − (𝟎. 𝟔𝟒 × 𝐚𝐠𝐞) What is the correct calculated target heart rate for the patient using the Karvonen method?
rationale: The correct math for this question begins with calculating the patient’s maximum heart rate with the provided equation: 0.64 × 19 = 12.16 211 − 12.16 = 198.84 The Karvonen method uses the following equation: Target HR = (% intensity × HRR) + Resting HR First, calculate the patient’s heart rate reserve (HRR) by subtracting resting heart rate from maximum heart rate: 198.84 − 61 = 137.84 Next, multiply the HRR by the desired intensity, which in this case is 80%: 137.84 × 0.80 = 110.27 Finally, add the resting heart rate: 110.27 + 61 = 171.27 Rounding to the nearest whole number results in 171 beats per minute as vigorous intensity for this patient.

stem: An athlete has experienced the sudden death of a family member in a car accident. A few teammates have told the athletic trainer that the athlete is dealing with her grief through excessive alcohol use. Which of the following could the athletic trainer use for further screening and assessment of the athlete? Select all that apply.
rationale: The main purpose of using screening tools is to identify factors that may put an athlete at risk. In this case, a wellness screening could be used to assess the athlete’s lifestyle behaviors, including alcohol use, and a mental health screening could be used to determine if further referral to a mental health professional is needed. A family medical history, orthopedic screening, or medications assessment would not provide information on the athlete’s current mental health status or lifestyle behaviors to determine if further intervention is needed.

stem: Which of the following situations requires a primary survey assessment? Select all that apply.
choices: A gymnast is unconscious after hitting his head on the bars.
A hockey player sustains a testicular contusion from getting hit by the puck and reports his pain as an 8 out of 10.
A cheerleader gets stung by a bee and goes into anaphylactic shock.
A football player collapses during practice and is not breathing.
A powerlifter says she heard a snap at her biceps while attempting a heavy lift.
A runner is forced to withdraw from a marathon due to severe muscle cramps.
rationale: Unconsciousness, shock, and not breathing are all potentially life-threatening situations that require a primary survey assessment; however, a primary survey assessment is not necessary if the injured individual is stable and conscious, as would be the case for a conscious individual who reports a high level of pain, a conscious individual who is able to relay to the athletic trainer what she heard or perceived in causing her injury, or a conscious individual experiencing muscle cramps. While care is necessary, these are not potentially life-threatening injuries or incidents.

stem: Which of the following is a common site for bursitis to occur? Select all that apply.
rationale: Bursitis can occur from trauma or repetitive movement and manifests as the bursa around a joint becoming irritated, swollen, and inflamed. The most common sites include the shoulder joint (subacromial bursa), elbow joint (olecranon bursa), knee joint (prepatellar bursa), and hip joint (trochanteric bursa). Bursa are located throughout the body, but the cervical spine or great toe are uncommon sites for bursitis to occur.

stem: Which of the following could be used as part of therapeutic interventions to rehabilitate injuries to the muscles of the hips or thighs? Select all that apply.
choices: Hamstring stretch on a stability ball
Prone extension stretch on the hands
rationale: General rehabilitation for hip or thigh muscle injuries should include stretching the muscles for all motions at the hip and thigh. These motions include flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, internal and external rotation, and circumduction. The soleus slant board stretch and the standing dorsiflexor stretch would both be used in cases of rehabilitation for the foot and ankle, rather than for the hips or thighs. The prone extension stretch on hands is used for low back rehabilitation, not specifically for hips or thighs.

stem: An athletic trainer is designing a rehabilitation program for a patient with a fibular fracture. Which of the following best illustrates the use of Wolff’s law in the patient’s rehabilitation program?
choices: The athletic trainer educates the patient on over-the-counter drugs to reduce pain and swelling.
The patient regularly uses an arm ergometer to avoid stress on the healing area.
The athletic trainer clearly explains the nature of the healing process to the patient.
The patient progresses to weight-bearing, full range-of-motion exercises as soon as possible.
The athletic trainer has the patient regularly rate their pain on a 1–10 scale.
rationale: The premise of Wolff’s law is that tissues such as bones will adapt to the stresses that are placed upon them and remodel accordingly. In the context of an injury rehabilitation program, the patient should progress to weight-bearing, full range-of-motion exercises as soon as possible. This would promote fibula remodeling in response to these demands, eventually resulting in attainment of full pre-injury strength and function. Conversely, if the healing area is immobilized or not fully used for a prolonged period, there will not be adequate stress for the fibula to remodel, resulting in decreased strength.

stem: Which of the following assessment tools would be used for evaluating concussion symptoms? Select all that apply.
rationale: There are a variety of tests that an athletic trainer may use to evaluate concussion symptoms. The SCAT-5 test (Sports Concussion Assessment Tool 5th edition) is the most widely used assessment to evaluate signs and symptoms of concussions and includes assessments of coordination, balance, and cognition. The VOMS (Vestibular/Ocular Motor Screening) assesses five domains of eye movements. The King-Devick test assesses eye movement and speed with comparisons to baseline measurements. The FADDIR is used to evaluate low back strain, and the Lever sign is used to evaluate ACL injury; neither are used for concussion symptoms. Tinel’s sign is used to evaluate nerve compromise in the posterior tibial nerve of the foot or in the ulnar nerve of the forearm.

stem: A patient is beginning a rehabilitation plan to recover from an injury. Which of the following are ways that the athletic trainer can promote a successful outcome? Select all that apply.
choices: Refer the patient to a physical therapist to create a rehabilitation plan.
Set long-term and short-term measurable goals.
Begin using dynamic functional activities within the first week after the injury.
Provide clear verbal and written explanations on the therapeutic exercises to be performed.
Ensure that rehabilitation minimizes unnecessary pain.
rationale: Setting goals, providing clear instruction, and minimizing pain are all ways to promote successful outcomes in rehabilitation from an injury. These will all help the patient stay compliant and motivated toward safely returning to activity. Creating a rehabilitation plan (also known as a plan of care) is within the professional scope of an athletic trainer, so referring the patient to a physical therapist is not necessary and could unnecessarily delay the beginning of the rehabilitation process. Dynamic functional activities would not be implemented until the later phase of healing.

stem: According to the SAID principle, which type of training would be most suitable for a college basketball player seeking to improve his speed and power on the court? Select all that apply.
rationale: The SAID principle stands for “specific adaptation to imposed demands.” For any desired adaptations in sports or athletic performance, the demands imposed on the body must be specific to create those adaptations. High-intensity interval training, or HIIT, involves alternating short, high-intensity work periods with active recovery periods and would be appropriate for the short bursts of speed needed on the basketball court. An effective HIIT approach for basketball would be sprinting work. Plyometric training involves the stretch-shortening cycle to generate explosive muscle movement and would be appropriate to improve jumping and passing power in basketball. This could be accomplished through vertical jump drills or medicine ball tosses. While the other choices could have some relevance to the training program, they are not directly applicable to creating speed and power adaptations in the body.

stem: Which of the following best describes the recommended percentage of total calories from protein, fat, and carbohydrates for most physically active adults?
rationale: It is important for everyone to eat a well-balanced, nutritious diet, but it is especially important for athletes or those who are physically active. Proper nutrition helps to effectively fuel the body and maximize endurance. The number of calories required by each athlete varies greatly based on body size, gender, activity level, and age. For most physically active adults, the appropriate percentage of calories is 55–60% from carbohydrates, 10–15% from protein, and no more than 30% from fat. The majority of carbohydrates should be complex carbohydrates from whole-grain breads and cereals rather than simple sugars. All carbohydrates break down to glucose and are used by the muscles for energy. Glucose that is not used for energy is stored as glycogen for use when needed. Protein is used for building muscle and repairing cells. Fat is also used as a source of energy as well as protecting organs and preventing excessive heat loss.

stem: A urinalysis may be useful in detecting which of the following? Select all that apply.
rationale: A urinalysis is typically a part of the pre-participation physical examination (PPE). As part of the PPE, legal permission is not required unless the urinalysis is being done as part of a drug testing program. A urinalysis can provide information on the presence of a urinary tract infection with an elevated white blood cell count. Blood or nitrates in the urine may also indicate infection, and an elevated urine specific gravity may indicate dehydration. A urinalysis can detect issues with the kidney or liver but is not commonly used to screen for bone issues. It can also detect the presence of glucose or ketones, both of which may signal diabetes. A urinalysis does not detect hypoglycemia.

stem: Which of the following is part of an emergency action plan for a sports team? Select all that apply.
choices: Retaining contact information and consent forms for all athletes
Deferring to the team physician for all medical emergencies that arise
Outlining the roles and responsibilities of each medical professional
Identifying the risks associated with the sport to determine the availability of emergency equipment
Assessing the yearly amount of funds used in the operating budget
rationale: Pre-planned details of an emergency action plan allow for a rapid and accurate response. Contact information and consent forms for all athletes should be on hand in case medical care is needed. Any personnel who are present during athletic activity (athletic trainer, coach, team physician, EMTs, etc.) should understand their role in response to an emergency so that the injured party receives care as quickly as possible. Identifying the risks of a given sport is also important so that proper emergency equipment is on hand. For example, tools for removal are necessary for sports with helmets and padding. While the team physician is an important part of the sports medicine team, the first person on the scene of an emergency (often the athletic trainer) should know how to respond and should not defer responsibility. The yearly amount of funding in the budget is part of an athletic trainer’s responsibility but is not part of emergency response.

stem: If an athlete with sickle cell disease experiences chest or abdominal pain and pain in the joints, they may be at immediate risk for which of the following complications?
rationale: Sickle cell disease is a hereditary disease commonly seen in individuals of African or Mediterranean descent. It involves abnormally shaped red blood cells that are severely affected by low oxygen levels. Sickle cell crisis involves extreme pain in the bones and joints. Symptoms include abdominal or chest pain, shortness of breath, rapid heart rate, fever, and fatigue. In some cases, a sickle cell crisis can progress to rhabdomyolysis. This can cause damage and even death to muscle tissue. If rhabdomyolysis occurs and is not treated, kidney failure can result. An athlete with sickle cell disease should ensure adequate hydration before, during, and after exercise. This helps to keep the urine diluted enough to flush out the dangerous toxins that may be present, preventing damage from occurring. Patients with sickle cell disease should be monitored closely at all times.

stem: Which of the following are recommendations provided by the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) for lightning safety? Select all that apply.
choices: One person should be designated to monitor the weather before and during sporting events and practices.
Be aware of the location of the closest safe structure where teams and spectators can seek shelter if necessary.
Cancel sporting events at the first sound of thunder.
If the time between lightning and thunder is thirty seconds or less, the athletic site should have already been evacuated and all individuals relocated to a shelter.
If thunder is heard but the sky is blue, there is no immediate danger, and play can continue until lightning is seen.
rationale: The development of a lightning safety plan is a necessary part of athletics. One person should be designated to monitor the weather and also to make the decision about suspending or cancelling an event. The nearest safe shelter location should be defined. This includes the nearest building that people normally use for business (i.e., not a shed or bathroom facility) or a vehicle with a hard roof. The presence of thunder with a blue sky can still be a source of danger because lightning can strike from as far as 10 miles away. The flash-to- bang (or lightning to thunder) count should be no less than 30 seconds. Once the 30 second count is reached, everyone should already be in a safe shelter or location. This is the equivalent of the storm being about six miles away. At least 30 minutes should elapse between the last flash of lightning and the last clap of thunder before resuming the sporting event.

stem: Which of the following findings on a female student athlete’s pre-participation examination would most likely impact her ability to begin training with the cross-country team?
choices: A blood pressure of 118/70 mm Hg
A heart rate of 60 beats per minute
A history of asthma that is controlled with medication
rationale: Evaluating a pre-participation examination should be done in conjunction with the patient’s physician. There are a number of conditions that would prohibit a patient from competition; however, it is at the physician’s discretion to make this decision. A history of asthma would not necessarily prohibit a patient from training or competing if it is well managed and medication is available. The blood pressure noted is in the optimal range, which is a systolic reading less than 120 mm Hg and a diastolic reading less than 80 mm Hg. A normal heart rate is 60–100 beats per minute (BPM), and some athletes in superb physical condition may have a heart rate as low as 40 BPM. The hematocrit, however, is low compared to a normal range of 34.9–44.5% for adult women and 38.8–50% for adult men. The hematocrit is the percentage of blood that is red blood cells. Hemoglobin is a type of protein found in red blood cells that helps transport oxygen. A low hematocrit, and therefore a low hemoglobin level, can lead to heart problems, because the heart may try to work harder to pump more oxygen throughout the body.

stem: What therapeutic intervention would be most appropriate for a patient recovering from a knee injury who is in the maturation-remodeling phase?
choices: Isometric exercise in an immobilized position
Aquatic exercise to maintain cardiorespiratory fitness
rationale: The maturation-remodeling phase is the third phase of healing following the acute inflammatory response phase and the fibroblastic repair phase. This phase can last the longest and has the primary focus of regaining full function and strength once inflammation, pain, and swelling are under control from care in the prior phases. Dynamic functional strengthening exercises are most appropriate at this stage to help restore coordination and neuromuscular control that will transfer into real-life activities. Compression and elevation are most appropriate during the acute inflammatory response phase to manage symptoms like pain and swelling. Isometric exercise in an immobilized position is also most appropriate during the early stages of rehabilitation when the injury needs to be protected. By the time of the maturation-remodeling phase, immobilization would not be appropriate, as it could pose a delay in regaining full function and strength. Aquatic exercise can be especially beneficial in the acute and fibroblastic repair phases to avoid excessive weight-bearing on the injured knee. It could also be viable as cross-training in maturation-remodeling, but the primary focus in this third phase of injury rehabilitation should be on using weight-bearing activities to regain full function. This cannot adequately be done with just aquatic exercise. Cryotherapy is typically used in the acute inflammatory response phase or the early stages of the fibroblastic repair phase as a method of cold application to reduce pain and inflammation.

stem: The athletic trainer begins responding by performing a primary survey assessment. What conditions is he looking for in a primary survey assessment? Select all that apply.
rationale: The priority in a primary survey is checking for life-threatening conditions that need immediate attention. These include unconsciousness, absence of breathing, severe bleeding, and shock. All of these are medical emergencies that require immediate intervention, such as activating EMS or starting CPR. Once critical conditions are ruled out with the primary survey, the athletic trainer can proceed to the secondary survey. While broken bones are important to address, they are typically identified during the secondary survey. Vomiting is not part of the primary survey unless it causes airway obstruction. A rapid heart rate indicates that the individual has circulation, so this would be most applicable to the secondary survey.
scenario: An athletic trainer is doing some yard work outside his home during a day off. Suddenly, he hears a crash and a shout from his neighbor’s backyard. Upon entering the scene, the athletic trainer sees his elderly neighbor lying supine on the ground near a tipped over ladder.

stem: The elderly neighbor is conscious and able to speak with the athletic trainer, but she is very pale and reports significant pain. The neighbor explains that she was climbing on the ladder to pick fruit from one of her fruit trees when she lost her balance and fell off the ladder, landing on her left hip. The athletic trainer observes that the left hip is in slight adduction and external rotation with muscle guarding. Based on all this information, the athletic trainer suspects a femoral fracture. He has a field kit nearby in his vehicle. How should the athletic trainer proceed?
choices: Splint the patient’s left hip and left knee.
Elevate the patient’s left leg to prevent shock.
Splint the patient’s left and right hips.
Splint the patient’s left hip, knee, and ankle as well as one side of the trunk.
Wait for EMS and then work together to lift the patient onto a spine board.
rationale: The correct approach for any suspected hip fracture is to splint the hip, knee, and ankle on the affected side as well as the trunk. This immobilizes the entire lower extremity, provides the most stability for a femoral fracture, and prevents further potential injury. Considering the athletic trainer has a field kit in his vehicle and is in a residential area where he could improvise supplies for a splint, splinting the injury immediately is a feasible first aid step that prevents further harm to the injured patient. Splinting the patient’s left hip and left knee is not sufficient for a suspected femoral fracture, and splinting both the left hip and right hip is not correct. Failure to properly immobilize a patient can cause harm, like tissue damage or hemorrhage. Elevating the injured leg is not correct, as the injury should be immobilized before making any attempt to move the patient. Similarly, it could cause harm to move the patient onto a spine board without immobilizing the injury.
scenario: An athletic trainer is doing some yard work outside his home during a day off. Suddenly, he hears a crash and a shout from his neighbor’s backyard. Upon entering the scene, the athletic trainer sees his elderly neighbor lying supine on the ground near a tipped over ladder.

stem: What special test would be most appropriate to determine whether the patient has a femoral fracture so that EMS can respond appropriately when they arrive?
rationale: Healthcare professionals use the patellar pubic percussion test to detect femoral fractures by tapping the patella while holding a stethoscope over the pubic tubercle. The fractured side will have a diminished sound compared to the non-injured side. The other listed tests are relevant to the hip and thigh but are not specific to detecting femoral fractures. The scour test is for detecting hip labrum tears or impingement. Ely’s test is used to evaluate the tightness of the rectus femoris. Craig’s test is used for measuring femoral anteversion. The Thomas test is used to detect the presence of hip contractures.
scenario: An athletic trainer is doing some yard work outside his home during a day off. Suddenly, he hears a crash and a shout from his neighbor’s backyard. Upon entering the scene, the athletic trainer sees his elderly neighbor lying supine on the ground near a tipped over ladder.

stem: Thanks to the athletic trainer’s rapid response, the patient got prompt emergency care and received a confirmed diagnosis of a femoral fracture. The patient underwent surgery and is now two months post-operation and is able to get around the house with a walker. At the last follow-up visit, the physician provided the patient with a questionnaire that included rating her quality of life and how effective she feels her care has been so far. This type of assessment is an example of which of the following?
rationale: Patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) are tools used to gather information directly from patients about aspects like their quality of life and the effectiveness of their care. These are typically structured questionnaires and can provide important insight about patient care and progression from healing injuries. The other listed options are used for other purposes, not patient-reported outcomes. Subjective, objective, assessment, plan (SOAP) is a method used to document patient encounters. Patient condition, interventions, comparison, outcome (PICO) is a framework used to formulate clinical research questions. The Strength of Recommendation Taxonomy (SORT) is used to grade the quality of evidence in clinical practice. The National Electronic Injury Surveillance System (NEISS) is a database used to track and analyze injury data from emergency departments.
scenario: An athletic trainer is doing some yard work outside his home during a day off. Suddenly, he hears a crash and a shout from his neighbor’s backyard. Upon entering the scene, the athletic trainer sees his elderly neighbor lying supine on the ground near a tipped over ladder.

stem: The patient is now six months post-operation and has been doing regular rehabilitation. What would be used in the patient’s rehabilitation to promote the regaining of neuromuscular control of the hip and lower body muscles? Select all that apply.
rationale: Neuromuscular control is essential for functional activities and is developed through combinations of postural alignment, balance, stability, and strength. Using exercises for balance, such as standing on unstable surfaces or walking in balance shoes, or closed kinetic chain exercises for strength, such as stability ball wall slides, all help restore neuromuscular control. Passive exercise or therapy does not promote neuromuscular control. This includes manual therapy techniques, such as femoral glides, or modalities used for pain or swelling, such as thermal ultrasound or intermittent compression. Proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation (PNF) is a flexibility technique and does not primarily target neuromuscular control. Isokinetic exercise uses a machine to move muscles at a constant speed, enhancing strength but not specifically neuromuscular control.
scenario: An athletic trainer is doing some yard work outside his home during a day off. Suddenly, he hears a crash and a shout from his neighbor’s backyard. Upon entering the scene, the athletic trainer sees his elderly neighbor lying supine on the ground near a tipped over ladder.

stem: An athletic trainer is out walking her dog in a park after work one day when she sees a jogger collapse a short distance ahead of her. The athletic trainer finds the jogger unresponsive, immediately activates EMS, and begins to provide CPR until EMS arrives. The athletic trainer’s actions save the jogger’s life, but the jogger sustains some bruised ribs from the CPR process. What provides the athletic trainer with limited protection from legal liability in this scenario?
rationale: Good Samaritan laws provide legal protection to individuals who voluntarily provide emergency care to those in need without expectation of compensation. These laws are designed to encourage bystanders to assist in emergencies without fear of being sued for unintentional injuries that may occur while providing aid. In this scenario, the athletic trainer acted in good faith to save the jogger's life. Any injuries sustained during the CPR process, such as bruised ribs, are considered a result of the necessary and appropriate emergency care provided. The jogger's health insurance may cover medical expenses related to the incident, but it does not provide legal protection for the athletic trainer. Health insurance is not related to liability protection in this context. Informed consent is a process where a patient agrees to a treatment plan after being informed of the risks, benefits, and alternatives. In emergency situations where the patient is unresponsive, obtaining informed consent is not feasible; therefore, informed consent is not relevant to this scenario. Liability insurance covers legal claims arising from professional services provided by the athletic trainer in their official capacity. In this scenario, the athletic trainer is acting as a bystander, not as part of her professional role, so her liability insurance does not apply. An indemnity plan is a type of insurance that is a traditional form of healthcare billing. It is not relevant to providing legal protection.

stem: A panel of athletic trainers each performed the same special test on an injured patient as part of a research study to investigate a new diagnostic technique. The majority of the athletic trainers had the same result for the test on the injured patient. This scenario best describes which of the following?
rationale: High inter-rater reliability refers to having a high level of agreement in results among different evaluators, which was the panel of athletic trainers in this case. Since the majority of them had the same result, this indicates that the test is useful because there is consistency and agreement when different people perform it. High test specificity indicates that the test has a high rate of correctly ruling in an injury. A positive likelihood ratio is the likelihood that a positive test correctly indicates that the condition is present. This measure is related to the diagnostic performance of the test and is calculated from sensitivity and specificity. Sensitivity refers to a test’s ability to rule out an injury. None of these three are the best description because they measure the diagnostic performance or accuracy of the test rather than the extent of agreement among different evaluators. Intra-rater reliability refers to the same evaluator performing the same test multiple times. Low intra-rater reliability would indicate that an individual did not get consistently similar results. This option is not the best description, because the scenario describes multiple athletic trainers (inter-rater) rather than a single one (intra-rater).

stem: During a college baseball game, a player is hit in the chest by a stray ball and becomes unresponsive. The athletic trainer is the first responder on the scene to provide care to the injured player. There is confusion among the coaching staff on who should call 911, who should get the AED, and who should manage the crowd. This confusion leads to a delay in the emergency response. Following the incident, the staff meets to review and improve the emergency action plan (EAP). What is the first priority the staff should clarify in the EAP to ensure a more efficient response in future emergencies?
choices: Type of emergency equipment needed for baseball
Roles and responsibilities of each team member during an emergency
Clear directions to the facility for the arrival of EMS
Current certifications in CPR, AED, and first aid for all sport staff members
Access to landlines whose locations are clearly marked
rationale: Clarifying and practicing the roles and responsibilities of each team member during an emergency is critical to ensuring a quick and effective response, minimizing confusion, and enhancing the chances of a positive outcome for the injured person. The other options are all important for emergency preparedness, but they do not directly address the need for clarification of the staff’s roles and responsibilities to prevent this type of issue from happening again.

stem: Which of the following would be an effective approach for the athletic trainer to promote adherence in a patient who is undergoing a long therapeutic rehabilitation process?
choices: Verbally explain all treatments and follow them up with written instructions.
Keep the exercise program the same each week so that the patient can develop mastery.
Rely on the patient’s peers or family to be sources of positive reinforcement.
Keep all exercises at a 5 or lower on a 1–10 pain scale.
Maintain a supportive, low-pressure environment by not setting specific goals.
rationale: Effective communication is a vital part of promoting adherence so that the patient knows and understands what is expected of them, such as doing exercises or therapies at home. Verbal instruction is important so that the patient feels informed, and written instructions help provide clarity so that the patient can remember what to do in their plan. While some consistency is beneficial, keeping the exercise program the same each week can lead to boredom and demotivation. Creativity and variation can help challenge the patient and keep the plan interesting and motivating. The patient’s peers or family should not be the sole source of positive reinforcement; the athletic trainer should also be a source of positive reinforcement in encouraging the patient on their progress. Most rehabilitation should be pain-free, whereas a 5 on a 1–10 pain scale is a significant level of discomfort. Patients are less likely to adhere if they expect that their treatment will typically be painful. Setting specific goals is a key element of promoting adherence because it gives the patient direction and motivation, so not setting goals would be counterproductive.

stem: In which of the following situations would it be most appropriate to use the upper quarter Y- balance test?
choices: To assess passive, active, and resistive motions in an older adult with frozen shoulder
To assess the presence of crepitation in a patient with a suspected patellar fracture
To assess the functional range of motion in a patient recovering from total knee replacement
To assess the presence of a pulse to evaluate nerve compression in an office worker with wrist pain
To assess dynamic upper extremity mobility and core stability in members of a volleyball team
rationale: The upper quarter Y-balance test is designed to evaluate dynamic upper extremity mobility and core stability. It uses a Y-Balance Test Kit and has the patient reach in varying directions with each arm while in a pushup position. Because both arms are compared, this can help screen for asymmetries that may increase the risk of injury. Out of the scenarios described, assessing the dynamic upper extremity mobility and core stability in members of a volleyball team would be the most appropriate use of this test. Assessing frozen shoulder would be more appropriate through tests specific to shoulder mobility and function, rather than through a dynamic test like the upper quarter Y-balance test. Since this test evaluates the upper body, it would not be appropriate to use it for lower body conditions like patellar fracture or recovering from total knee replacement. Evaluating the presence of a pulse for diagnosing nerve compression would be done with special tests like Tinel’s sign or Phalen’s test, not the upper quarter Y-balance test.

stem: A patient reports to the athletic trainer with back pain after twisting while trying to pick up a heavy box. What test could the athletic trainer use to evaluate whether the patient’s acute injury is specific to the lumbar spine?
rationale: The knees-to-chest test is used to assess the lumbar spine by having the patient bring their knees to their chest while lying supine. Pain indicates some type of lumbar pathology. Additionally, pain with a single knee pulled into the chest can indicate irritation to the ligaments of the lower back. Spurling’s test and the brachial plexus test are used for injuries to the cervical spine, not the lumbar spine. The vertebral artery test detects the presence of occlusion due to compression in the cervical spine. Craig’s test assesses femoral anteversion by measuring the femur’s angle to determine its extent of internal rotation.

stem: An athletic trainer is one of the on-staff employees at a large community-based recreational and gym facility. A participant joins a pickleball recreational sports league offered by the facility and signs a form acknowledging the potential risks of injury. During a pickleball match, the participant sustains a torn meniscus. The participant is now considering filing legal action against the facility. Which of the following best describes how assumption of risk affects the participant’s ability to pursue legal action?
choices: The signed form automatically protects the facility from any type of legal action.
Assumption of risk only applies if the participant verbally acknowledged that they understood the potential risks.
Since the participant got injured while at the facility, they can pursue legal action regardless of the signed form.
The participant assumed the risks associated with physical activity by signing the form, which is likely to limit their ability to pursue legal action.
Evidence of negligence is necessary in order for the participant to pursue any type of legal action.
rationale: The concept of assumption of risk is based on a participant acknowledging and accepting the inherent risks involved with participating in an activity, such as a physical activity like a pickleball league. By signing the form, the participant has given voluntary consent to accept the potential for these risks, which can limit their ability to pursue legal action. While the signed form provides some level of protection for the facility by indicating that the participant understood and accepted the risk, it is not an automatic absolute protection. For example, if negligence were found, the facility would not be automatically protected. Assumption of risk is typically established through written consent so that there is a signed record rather than a verbal acknowledgement. A signed form is generally considered sufficient evidence of assumption of risk, as it assumes that the participant has read the form before signing. While the participant could pursue legal action, the signed form can limit their ability to succeed in a legal case. Evidence of negligence is not a requirement for a participant to pursue legal action, though it could strengthen their case if it were present; however, this does not best describe the effects of the participant consenting to assumption of risk.

stem: The head football coach and the athletic trainer at a high school are doing an equipment inventory at the end of the season. They find a few helmets left over from several years ago, but the helmets are unused and still in their boxes. Before deciding to use these helmets for practice, they want to ensure that this equipment meets safety standards. Which organization’s standards would be consulted for this purpose?
choices: National Operating Committee on Standards for Athletic Equipment (NOCSAE)
National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) Injury Surveillance System
National Center for Catastrophic Sports Injury Research (NCCSIR)
rationale: The National Operating Committee on Standards for Athletic Equipment (NOCSAE) is responsible for establishing test standards and safety requirements for athletic equipment, such as helmets, for football, baseball, softball, and lacrosse. Confirming that the helmets are NOCSAE-approved and that they meet their requirements for performance will ensure that they can be used safely. All other organizations listed do not have roles that are specific to equipment safety. The National Electronic Injury Surveillance System (NEISS) collects data on injury trends from consumer products. The National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) Injury Surveillance System collects and analyzes data from NCAA sports and would not be relevant to a high school setting. The National Athletic Trainers' Association (NATA) provides resources and support for athletic trainers. As its name suggests, the National Center for Catastrophic Injury Research (NCCSIR) compiles data about catastrophic injuries across sports environments.

stem: Which of the following scenarios best illustrates implied consent to treat in an emergency situation? Select all that apply.
choices: An athlete who is unconscious after sustaining a severe head injury
A youth athlete who sustains a deep laceration and whose parents insist on providing first aid themselves
An athlete with nut allergies who asks for the athletic trainer’s help administering her EpiPen after she starts to feel dizzy during a team meal
An athlete who is unresponsive from a suspected drug overdose
An athlete with hypertension who requests aspirin due to experiencing unusual chest pain
An athlete with diabetes who verbally declines treatment for his hypoglycemia, insisting he can handle it himself
rationale: Implied consent to treat applies when a person is unable to provide consent to treat due to their condition and immediate action is necessary to prevent harm or save their life. In the cases of an unconscious athlete with a severe head injury and an unresponsive athlete suffering from a suspected drug overdose, neither is conscious to give consent and immediate care is necessary because these are life-threatening situations. Implied consent does not apply in the other listed situations. Either the athlete is a minor and the parents are taking over treatment of the injury, the athlete is explicitly requesting assistance (which is a form of expressed consent), or the athlete is explicitly refusing treatment, which means that implied consent is not applicable.

stem: A patient recovering from a triangular fibrocartilage complex (TFCC) injury has gone through four weeks of immobilization and will now be starting range of motion and strengthening exercises with the athletic trainer. One of the goals in the patient’s plan of care is to regain normative range of motion at the wrist. What would be the most effective way to measure progress toward this goal?
choices: Measuring wrist girth with a tape measure
rationale: A goniometer is a tool that measures joint range of motion by using a stationary arm and a movable arm to assess joint angle. In this situation, the athletic trainer would be able to use objective goniometer measurements over time to assess the patient’s progress toward regaining normative range of motion. Sensory testing assesses nerve function and sensation, not information about range of motion. Self-reported pain levels are important, but they are subjective and would not provide the objective range of motion measurements that indicate progress. Palpation would be used to assess abnormalities or tenderness, but it would not provide measurable insight about range of motion. Measuring wrist girth could be used for assessing changes in swelling or muscle mass, but it would not be the most effective option for measuring range of motion.

stem: An ice hockey player is shoved headfirst into the boards when a fight between the teams breaks out. The player is lying unresponsive on the ice as the athletic trainer approaches. The injured athlete is wearing a helmet with a facemask, shoulder pads, thorax protector, elbow pads, gloves, athletic cup, shin pads, and ice skates. The athletic trainer suspects a possible cervical spine injury. What is the most appropriate initial action to take regarding the removal of equipment to provide care?
choices: Remove the shoulder pads as preparation to slide the injured athlete onto a spine board.
Stabilize the head and neck while another rescuer prepares for helmet removal.
Remove only the facemask as preparation for providing rescue breaths.
Remove the thorax protector in case an AED is needed.
Leave all equipment on for transport by EMS to minimize harm caused by removing it.
rationale: The current consensus on equipment removal in an emergency situation is that appropriate removal of protective equipment should be done before transport by EMS. With a suspected cervical spine injury, removing the helmet would be the most appropriate initial action. This would require two rescuers. One should remove the helmet while the other stabilizes the cervical spine. While the shoulder pads should also be removed, access to the athlete’s head and face takes priority. The helmet should be removed first so that the athletic trainer can provide care, like airway management. Removing only the facemask but leaving the helmet on would not be the most appropriate step. While removing the thorax protector would be necessary if an AED is required, the immediate priority is stabilizing the spine while removing the helmet. Leaving the equipment on is not the preferred option for the most effective care, as it could delay response in addressing the athlete’s injury.

stem: An athletic trainer reads on the local news website that there is a potential outbreak of meningitis in his area. What symptoms should the athletic trainer be aware of that would indicate meningitis?
choices: Tingling in the extremities, irritability, and shallow respiration
Decreased physical performance, nausea, and craving ice
Stiff neck, headache, fever, and light sensitivity
Sore throat, wheezing, and coughing with mucus secretions
rationale: Meningitis is characterized by symptoms such as stiff neck, headache, fever, and light sensitivity. It is caused by inflammation of the meninges around the brain and spinal cord and is contracted from a virus or bacteria spread through respiratory droplets. Because meningitis is highly contagious, anyone with these symptoms should be isolated from others as part of their treatment and recovery. The other options listed are more indicative of conditions other than meningitis, such as insulin shock, iron-deficiency anemia, some types of sexually transmitted infections, and bronchitis, respectively.

stem: An athletic trainer is providing a pre-season educational talk to a group of college coaches on the topic of preventing anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) injuries. Which of the following strategies should be included in the educational talk? Select all that apply.
choices: Use cleated shoes with longer cleats to provide more traction on surfaces.
Incorporate properly programmed plyometric exercises into athlete workouts.
Emphasize both strength and flexibility for the muscles that act on the knee joint.
Instruct athletes to train at lower intensities if they have sustained a mild knee injury.
Use elements of proprioception and balance in athlete workouts.
Ensure a strength ratio where the hamstrings are stronger than the quadriceps.
rationale: Plyometric exercises that focus on proper technique and body mechanics have been shown to be preventative for anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) injuries. These also emphasize both the strength and flexibility of the muscles that act on the knee joint, such as the quadriceps, hamstrings, and gastrocnemius. Using elements of proprioception and balance is also preventative, as these improve the body’s ability to sense and control its movement, reducing the risk of injury. Shoes with appropriate traction for the sport are important, but not all sports need long cleats. Longer cleats can increase ACL injury risk by putting higher rotational traction forces on the knee. Athletes who have sustained knee injuries are at greater risk for re- injury, so even mild knee injuries should go through proper rehabilitation, not just training at reduced intensities. A balanced strength ratio is important; however, it is recommended that the quadriceps be stronger than the hamstrings, not the other way around. Hamstring strength should be about 60–70% of quadriceps strength.

stem: Which of the following most accurately reflects the proper procedure for evaluating a patient by using the Trendelenburg test?
choices: Have the patient lie supine and raise the legs to 90 degrees; observe for any pelvic tilt or asymmetry.
Have the patient squat to a 90-degree angle; observe for knee valgus or varus movement.
Have the patient stand on one leg and then perform a calf raise; observe for any imbalance or inability to lift the heel.
Have the patient sit on the edge of a table and extend both legs; apply downward pressure to assess hip flexor strength.
